title: Intersection of Two Linked Lists
hints: If both lists have the same length, they would intersect at the same step. How can you equalize the lengths?
Advance the pointer on the longer list by the difference in lengths, then walk both pointers together.
Alternatively, when a pointer reaches the end, redirect it to the head of the other list — they'll meet at the intersection after at most m+n steps.
approach: Calculate lengths of both lists. Advance the pointer on the longer list by the length difference. Then walk both pointers together until they meet — that's the intersection node. Returns null if no intersection.
time_complexity_explanation: Both pointers traverse at most m+n nodes before meeting.
space_complexity_explanation: Only two pointer variables are used.
key_insight: Redirecting each pointer to the other list's head after reaching the end equalizes the total path length, guaranteeing they meet at the intersection.

title: Remove Duplicates from Sorted List
hints: Since the list is sorted, duplicates are always adjacent — you only need to compare each node with its immediate next.
If current.val == current.next.val, skip the next node by pointing current.next to current.next.next.
Continue until you reach the end of the list — no extra data structure needed.
approach: Traverse the sorted linked list with a single pointer. Whenever the current node's value equals the next node's value, skip the next node. Otherwise, advance the pointer. Continue until the end of the list.
time_complexity_explanation: Each node is visited exactly once during the single traversal.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a single pointer variable is maintained; no extra space is used.
key_insight: Because the list is sorted, all duplicate values are contiguous, so a simple linear scan can remove them without needing a hash set.

title: Separate Squares I
hints: For a given horizontal line at height y, compute the total area of each square that lies above vs. below the line.
For a square with bottom-left (xi, yi) and side s: area below = min(s, max(0, y - yi)) * s; area above = max(0, yi + s - y) * s.
Binary search on y between the minimum and maximum y-coordinate values; the function is monotone so you can converge to the answer.
approach: Binary search on the y-coordinate of the horizontal line. For each candidate y, compute total area above and below by iterating over all squares. The area-above function is monotonically decreasing in y, so binary search converges. Return the y where both halves are equal.
time_complexity_explanation: Binary search runs ~100 iterations; each iteration costs O(n) to evaluate all squares.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of extra variables are used regardless of input size.
key_insight: The total area above minus below is a monotonically decreasing function of y, enabling precise binary search to find the balancing line.

title: Employees Earning More Than Their Managers
hints: Self-join the Employee table so you can compare an employee's salary to their manager's salary in the same row.
Join on e1.managerId = e2.id to pair each employee with their manager.
Filter where e1.salary > e2.salary to find employees who out-earn their managers.
approach: Perform a self-join on the Employee table, matching each employee to their manager via managerId = id. Filter rows where the employee's salary exceeds the manager's salary and select the employee's name.
solution_python:
# SQL Solution:
# SELECT e1.name AS Employee
# FROM Employee e1
# JOIN Employee e2 ON e1.managerId = e2.id
# WHERE e1.salary > e2.salary
solution_js:
/* SQL Solution:
SELECT e1.name AS Employee
FROM Employee e1
JOIN Employee e2 ON e1.managerId = e2.id
WHERE e1.salary > e2.salary
*/
time_complexity_explanation: The self-join in the worst case compares every employee pair; indexes on id/managerId reduce this in practice.
space_complexity_explanation: The result set can hold up to O(n) rows in the worst case.
key_insight: A self-join lets you treat the same table as two separate entities — employees and managers — to directly compare salary values within a single query.

title: Group Shifted Strings
hints: Two strings belong to the same shift group if one can be obtained from the other by shifting all characters by the same amount.
Normalize each string by computing the difference between consecutive characters modulo 26 — this becomes the canonical key.
Group all strings with the same canonical key together using a hash map.
approach: For each string, compute the tuple of differences between consecutive character ASCII values modulo 26. This tuple is invariant under shifts and serves as a group key. Use a hash map to collect all strings sharing the same key, then return the grouped lists.
time_complexity_explanation: Each of the n strings of average length k is processed once to compute its key.
space_complexity_explanation: The hash map stores all n strings along with their keys of length O(k).
key_insight: The sequence of character differences modulo 26 is invariant under cyclic shifts, providing a canonical fingerprint for each equivalence class.

title: Minimize Maximum Pair Sum in Array
hints: To minimize the maximum pair sum, you want to avoid pairing two large numbers together.
Sort the array — then pair the smallest element with the largest, the second smallest with the second largest, and so on.
After sorting, the optimal pairing is nums[i] + nums[n-1-i] for i from 0 to n/2-1; take the maximum of these.
approach: Sort the array in ascending order. Use two pointers starting at the two ends, moving inward. Pair nums[left] with nums[right] and track the maximum pair sum. Return that maximum — this greedy strategy provably minimizes it.
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting dominates the runtime; the subsequent linear scan is O(n).
space_complexity_explanation: Sorting is done in place and only a constant number of extra variables are used.
key_insight: Pairing the smallest with the largest element minimizes the worst-case pair sum; any other pairing would push two large values together, increasing the maximum.

title: Find And Replace in String
hints: Processing replacements left-to-right shifts indices for later replacements. Process right-to-left to avoid index drift.
Sort the replacement operations by index in descending order, then apply each: if s[index:index+len(source)] == source, replace with target.
Alternatively, record which indices are valid replacement starts first, then build the result string in one pass.
approach: Sort the replacement operations by index in descending order. For each operation, check if the substring at that index matches the source. If it does, splice in the target. Processing right-to-left ensures earlier replacements don't invalidate later indices.
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting the m operations is O(m log m); applying them to the string of length n is O(n + total replacement length).
space_complexity_explanation: The string is converted to a character array of size O(n) for in-place splicing.
key_insight: Right-to-left processing of replacements keeps all previously computed indices valid, avoiding the need for offset bookkeeping.

title: Move Pieces to Obtain a String
hints: L pieces can only move left; R pieces can only move right. Underscores are empty spaces.
The relative order of L and R pieces cannot change — if the sequence of non-underscore chars differs between start and target, return false.
For each matched pair of L/R characters, check movement direction validity: an L cannot move right (its index must be >= target index), an R cannot move left.
approach: Use two pointers to iterate over start and target, skipping underscores. At each step, the non-underscore characters must match. For an L piece, its starting index must be >= its target index (it can only move left). For an R piece, its starting index must be <= its target index. Any violation returns false.
solution_python:
class Solution:
    def canChange(self, start: str, target: str) -> bool:
        n = len(start)
        i = j = 0
        while i <= n and j <= n:
            while i < n and start[i] == '_': i += 1
            while j < n and target[j] == '_': j += 1
            if i == n and j == n: return True
            if i == n or j == n: return False
            if start[i] != target[j]: return False
            if start[i] == 'L' and i < j: return False
            if start[i] == 'R' and i > j: return False
            i += 1; j += 1
        return True
time_complexity_explanation: Each pointer advances at most n steps through the strings.
space_complexity_explanation: Only two pointer indices are maintained regardless of string length.
key_insight: Because L and R pieces cannot pass through each other, their relative order is fixed. The only question is whether each piece can reach its target position given its movement constraint.

title: Minimum Cost Path with Teleportations
hints: Model each cell as a node in a weighted graph. Adjacent cells have edge weight 1.
Portal pairs have edge weight 0 or 1 (depending on problem variant). Add teleportation edges between portal pairs.
Run Dijkstra's algorithm from (0,0); the shortest distance to (n-1,n-1) is the answer.
approach: Build a weighted graph where each grid cell is a node. Add edges of weight 1 for cardinal moves. For each portal pair, add a bidirectional edge of weight 0 (or 1) to represent teleportation. Run Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm from (0,0) to (n-1,n-1).
time_complexity_explanation: Dijkstra's runs on a graph with n^2 nodes and E edges (grid edges + portal edges).
space_complexity_explanation: The graph adjacency list and distance array both scale with the number of nodes and edges.
key_insight: Teleportation portals are simply zero-weight edges in the graph; Dijkstra's naturally exploits them to find the globally cheapest path.

title: Minimum Cost to Convert String I
hints: You need the minimum cost to convert any character a to any character b. Model the 26 lowercase letters as nodes in a weighted directed graph.
Apply Floyd-Warshall to find the shortest (minimum cost) path between every pair of characters.
For each position where source[i] != target[i], add the precomputed minimum conversion cost. If any conversion is impossible, return -1.
approach: Build a 26x26 cost matrix from the replacement rules. Run Floyd-Warshall to compute the minimum cost for every character-to-character conversion. Then for each index where source and target differ, look up the precomputed minimum cost and accumulate the total. Return -1 if any conversion is unreachable.
time_complexity_explanation: Floyd-Warshall on 26 nodes runs in O(26^3) = O(1) constant time; processing the string is O(n).
space_complexity_explanation: The 26x26 distance matrix is the dominant space usage.
key_insight: Floyd-Warshall on just 26 character nodes precomputes all-pairs shortest conversion costs efficiently, reducing the per-position lookup to O(1).

title: Find Nth Smallest Integer With K One Bits
hints: The count of integers up to x with exactly k set bits can be computed using combinatorics over the binary representation of x.
Use binary search on the answer: find the smallest number x such that count(x, k) >= n.
To count integers up to x with exactly k set bits, process the binary digits of x from the most significant bit using a digit DP or combinatorial approach.
approach: Binary search on the answer value. For each candidate x, count how many non-negative integers <= x have exactly k bits set using a combinatorial digit-DP approach (choose remaining bits from remaining positions). Find the smallest x where this count equals n.
time_complexity_explanation: Binary search over O(log N) candidates, each requiring O(log N) steps for the digit-DP count.
space_complexity_explanation: The digit-DP uses O(log N) space for processing binary digits.
key_insight: Combinatorics lets you count integers with exactly k set bits up to a value x in O(log x) time, making binary search on the Nth value feasible.

title: Find Missing Elements
hints: Load all elements of the array into a hash set for O(1) lookup.
Iterate over every integer in [lo, hi]; if it's not in the set, it's missing.
Collect all missing integers into a result list and return it.
approach: Insert all array elements into a hash set. Then iterate from lo to hi inclusive and collect every integer not present in the set. Return the collected list of missing integers.
time_complexity_explanation: Building the set is O(n); iterating the range is O(hi - lo + 1).
space_complexity_explanation: The hash set stores all n elements of the input array.
key_insight: A hash set provides O(1) membership tests, making the range scan efficient regardless of array size or value distribution.

title: Array Partition
hints: Think about which pairs maximize the sum of minimums — pairing the largest with the second largest wastes potential.
Sort the array: pairing adjacent elements (index 0&1, 2&3, ...) means the minimum of each pair is the smaller element at the even index.
After sorting, summing elements at indices 0, 2, 4, ... gives the maximum possible sum of pair minimums.
approach: Sort the array in ascending order. Then, pair elements at consecutive indices (0,1), (2,3), etc. The minimum of each pair is always the element at the even index. Summing all elements at even indices after sorting yields the maximum sum of minimums.
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting dominates at O(n log n); the subsequent sum scan is O(n).
space_complexity_explanation: Sorting is done in-place and only a constant amount of extra variables are used.
key_insight: Sorting and pairing adjacent elements guarantees that the wasted element (the larger of each pair) is as small as possible, maximizing the sum of the minimums.

title: Max Consecutive Ones III
hints: Use a sliding window with two pointers. Expand the right pointer and track how many zeros are in the window.
When the number of zeros exceeds k, shrink the window from the left until the zero count is back to k.
The answer is the maximum window size seen across all valid windows.
approach: Maintain a sliding window with left and right pointers. Expand right and decrement k when a 0 is encountered. When k drops below 0, advance left (and restore k if left was a 0). Track the maximum window width throughout.
time_complexity_explanation: Each element is visited at most twice (once by right pointer, once by left pointer).
space_complexity_explanation: Only pointer variables and a counter are maintained; no extra data structures.
key_insight: The window never shrinks below its maximum size — the left pointer only advances when necessary, so the final window size equals the maximum valid window found.

title: Greatest Common Divisor of Strings
hints: A string t divides s if s = t + t + ... (t repeated some number of times).
If a common divisor exists, then str1 + str2 must equal str2 + str1 (concatenation in both orders).
If they are equal, the GCD string is the prefix of str1 with length gcd(len(str1), len(str2)).
approach: Check if str1 + str2 equals str2 + str1. If not, no common divisor exists and return ''. If yes, the answer is the prefix of length gcd(len(str1), len(str2)) — this is the longest string that divides both.
time_complexity_explanation: The concatenation check is O(m+n); the GCD computation is O(log(min(m,n))).
space_complexity_explanation: The concatenated strings for comparison each require O(m+n) space.
key_insight: If a GCD string exists, both concatenation orders must be identical — this elegant check is both necessary and sufficient.

title: Trionic Array I
hints: A trionic array can be split into groups of 3 consecutive equal elements — so each distinct value must appear a multiple of 3 times.
Count the frequency of every element. If any frequency is not divisible by 3, it's impossible.
If all frequencies are divisible by 3, sort the array and verify groups of 3 consecutive elements are equal.
approach: Count the frequency of each element using a hash map. If any frequency is not divisible by 3, return false. Otherwise, sort the array and greedily verify that every group of three consecutive elements are equal. Return true if all groups are valid.
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting the array dominates; frequency counting and group verification are both O(n).
space_complexity_explanation: The frequency map stores at most n entries in the worst case.
key_insight: A necessary and sufficient condition is that every element's frequency is divisible by 3 AND the sorted array has equal triplets at every group of three consecutive positions.

title: X of a Kind in a Deck of Cards
hints: Count the frequency of each card value. Any valid X must divide every frequency.
The set of valid X values is exactly the set of common divisors of all frequencies.
The GCD of all frequencies is the largest such divisor. If GCD >= 2, a valid X exists.
approach: Count the frequency of each distinct card value. Compute the GCD of all frequencies. If the GCD is 2 or more, a valid grouping exists (X = GCD works). Otherwise return false.
solution_python:
from collections import Counter
from math import gcd
from functools import reduce
class Solution:
    def hasGroupsSizeX(self, deck: List[int]) -> bool:
        counts = Counter(deck).values()
        return reduce(gcd, counts) >= 2
time_complexity_explanation: Frequency counting is O(n); GCD computation over all frequencies is O(k log C) where k is the number of distinct values and C is the max frequency.
space_complexity_explanation: The frequency map stores one entry per distinct card value.
key_insight: Any valid X must divide all frequencies simultaneously, so the GCD of all frequencies is both necessary and sufficient to check for a valid grouping.

title: Russian Doll Envelopes
hints: Sort envelopes by width ascending. For envelopes with the same width, sort by height descending — this prevents using two envelopes of the same width.
After sorting, the problem reduces to finding the Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS) on heights only.
Use patience sorting with binary search to find LIS in O(n log n).
approach: Sort envelopes by width ascending, breaking ties by height descending. Run the O(n log n) LIS algorithm on heights: maintain a 'tails' array and binary search for each height's position. The length of 'tails' at the end is the answer.
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting is O(n log n); the LIS with binary search is also O(n log n).
space_complexity_explanation: The 'tails' array holds at most n elements in the worst case.
key_insight: Sorting by width ascending and height descending for equal widths prevents falsely using multiple envelopes of the same width, reducing the 2D problem to a standard 1D LIS.

title: Range Module
hints: Maintain a sorted list of disjoint intervals. Each operation (add/remove/query) finds relevant intervals using binary search.
For addRange: find all intervals overlapping [left, right), merge them into one expanded interval.
For removeRange: find all intervals overlapping [left, right), split or trim their endpoints. For queryRange: check no gaps exist in [left, right).
approach: Store disjoint intervals in a sorted list. addRange merges all overlapping/adjacent intervals into one. removeRange trims or splits existing intervals, possibly creating two new ones. queryRange checks whether the entire [left, right) is covered by a single existing interval.
time_complexity_explanation: Each operation may scan or modify all n stored intervals in the worst case; with a sorted structure, add/remove are O(n) due to shifting.
space_complexity_explanation: The interval list stores at most O(Q) intervals where Q is the number of add/remove operations.
key_insight: Maintaining a canonical list of non-overlapping intervals allows all three operations to be implemented by carefully merging, splitting, and checking intervals around the target range.

title: Trionic Array II
hints: Think about what it costs to make each group of three consecutive positions equal to some target value.
For every triple of positions, compute the cost of setting all three to each possible value (the cheapest is to pick the value requiring fewest changes).
Use DP where dp[i] = minimum operations to make the first i elements trionic. Transition considers all triples ending at position i.
approach: Partition the array into consecutive groups of three. For each group, the minimum cost to make all three equal is (3 minus the count of the most frequent element in the group). Use DP to accumulate these minimum costs across all groups, returning the total minimum operations.
time_complexity_explanation: Each element is processed exactly once across all groups; each group of 3 is processed in O(1) time.
space_complexity_explanation: Each group's frequency map has at most 3 entries, so space usage is constant.
key_insight: Within each group of three consecutive positions, it's always optimal to keep the most frequent element and change the others — minimizing the number of operations per group independently minimizes the global total.

title: Last Day Where You Can Still Cross
hints: Binary search on the day. For a given day, check if you can cross from top to bottom row.
For the check: BFS/DFS from any land cell in row 0, see if you reach row n-1.
Alternatively, use reverse Union-Find: start from the last day and add land cells back, connecting to virtual top/bottom nodes.
approach: Binary search on the day. For a given day d, build the grid with all cells flooded up to day d, then BFS/DFS to check if there is a path from top row to bottom row through land cells.
time_complexity_explanation: Binary search over O(row*col) days, each check is O(row*col) BFS.
space_complexity_explanation: Grid storage is O(row*col).
key_insight: Binary search on the answer: the latest valid day is monotone — if you can cross on day d, you can cross on all days before d.

title: Minimum Removals to Balance Array
hints: Think of '(' as +1 and ')' as -1; a balanced array needs total sum 0 and all prefix sums >= 0.
For each index as a split point, count excess ')' on the left and excess '(' on the right, summing gives min removals.
Use prefix arrays: left_close[i] = unmatched ')' in prefix, right_open[i] = unmatched '(' in suffix.
approach: Map '(' to +1 and ')' to -1. Precompute unmatched closing brackets from the left and unmatched opening brackets from the right. For each possible split, the answer is left_close[i] + right_open[i]. Return the minimum over all splits.
time_complexity_explanation: Two linear passes to build prefix arrays plus one pass to find the minimum.
space_complexity_explanation: Two prefix arrays of length n+1 are stored.
key_insight: Treat unmatched ')' as removals from the left and unmatched '(' as removals from the right; sweeping a split point gives the globally optimal removal count.

title: Divisor Game
hints: Try small values: n=1 loses, n=2 wins, n=3 loses, n=4 wins — the pattern is whether n is even or odd.
If n is even, Alice picks x=1 (odd-1=even isn't required; she hands Bob an odd number), forcing Bob into a losing position.
Prove by induction: even always wins, odd always loses — so the answer is simply n % 2 == 0.
approach: Alice wins if and only if n is even. When n is even she picks x=1, leaving Bob with n-1 (odd). All divisors of an odd number are odd, so Bob is always stuck with an even-minus-odd = odd number, perpetuating the losing pattern.
time_complexity_explanation: A single modulo operation.
space_complexity_explanation: No extra space needed.
key_insight: The game reduces to parity: even n is always a winning position and odd n is always losing, provable by induction.

title: Sort Transformed Array
hints: The quadratic f(x)=ax²+bx+c is a parabola — its extreme values are at the ends of the sorted input.
Use two pointers at both ends of the sorted array. For a>0 the largest values come from the ends, so fill result from right; for a<0 the smallest come from the ends, fill from left.
When a==0 the function is linear; two pointers still work correctly using the same logic.
approach: Sort the input, then use two pointers (lo, hi). Evaluate f(nums[lo]) and f(nums[hi]). If a>=0 place the larger value at the back of result; if a<0 place the smaller at the front. Advance the corresponding pointer.
time_complexity_explanation: Single two-pointer pass after the array is already sorted (O(n log n) if sorting is included).
space_complexity_explanation: Output array of size n.
key_insight: A parabola's extreme values lie at the endpoints of any sorted interval, so two pointers can assemble the sorted output in a single pass.

title: Find the Smallest Divisor Given a Threshold
hints: As the divisor increases the sum of ceil(nums[i]/divisor) decreases — this monotonicity enables binary search.
Binary search on divisor in [1, max(nums)]. For each mid, compute the ceiling division sum.
Use integer ceiling division: ceil(a/b) == (a + b - 1) // b. Return the smallest divisor where sum <= threshold.
approach: Binary search on the divisor d in range [1, max(nums)]. For each candidate d compute sum of ceil(nums[i]/d) for all i. If the sum is <= threshold, d might be valid — try smaller. Otherwise try larger. Return the leftmost valid d.
time_complexity_explanation: Binary search over max(nums) values, each requiring an O(n) sum computation.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of variables are used.
key_insight: The sum function is monotonically decreasing in the divisor, so binary search directly finds the smallest valid divisor.

title: Bus Routes
hints: Model the problem as BFS where each 'node' is a bus route, not a stop — this keeps the state space manageable.
Build a map from each stop to all routes that pass through it. Starting from the source stop, enqueue all routes touching it.
Expand route by route: for each new route, visit all its stops; enqueue any unvisited routes that share those stops. Count route-level hops.
approach: Build a stop-to-routes map. BFS on routes: seed the queue with all routes containing the source stop, count = 1. For each dequeued route visit all its stops; if target found return count. For each stop enqueue all unseen routes and mark them visited. Increment count per BFS level.
time_complexity: O(N) where N is total stops across all routes
time_complexity_explanation: Each stop and each route is processed at most once during BFS.
space_complexity_explanation: The stop-to-routes map and visited sets together store all stops and routes.
key_insight: BFS at the route level (not stop level) avoids revisiting entire routes and naturally counts the minimum number of bus transfers.

title: Longest Non-Decreasing Subarray After Replacing at Most One Element
hints: Track two DP values per index: dp0[i] = longest non-decreasing subarray ending at i with no replacement, dp1[i] = with exactly one replacement.
dp0[i] extends dp0[i-1] when nums[i] >= nums[i-1]; dp1[i] can extend dp0[i-1] unconditionally (use the replacement here) or extend dp1[i-1] when nums[i] >= nums[i-1].
The answer is the maximum of all dp0 and dp1 values.
approach: Use two DP arrays: dp0[i] for the longest non-decreasing subarray ending at i without any replacement, and dp1[i] allowing exactly one replacement. Transition dp1[i] by either extending dp0[i-1] (place replacement at i) or extending dp1[i-1] if nums[i] >= nums[i-1]. Track the global maximum.
time_complexity_explanation: Single linear pass over the array.
space_complexity_explanation: Only two DP scalar variables are maintained.
key_insight: Separating the DP into 'zero replacements used' and 'one replacement used' states allows transitions in O(1) per element.

title: Guess the Word
hints: After each guess you get the number of exact character matches. Filter remaining candidates to those with the same match count against your guess.
To minimize the worst case, pick the next guess that maximizes the minimum number of words eliminated regardless of the response.
A simpler heuristic: pick the word that has the most occurrences of the most common character per position — this tends to eliminate many candidates quickly.
approach: Maintain a candidate list. On each turn, pick a candidate (e.g., randomly or by minimax heuristic), call guess(), receive match count, then filter the candidate list to words with exactly that many matching characters against the guessed word. Repeat until match count is 6.
time_complexity_explanation: Each filtering step is O(n) comparisons of length-6 strings; up to 10 rounds are performed.
space_complexity_explanation: Candidate list of up to n words is maintained.
key_insight: After each guess, only words with the same match count can be the secret — this exponentially shrinks the candidate pool with each well-chosen guess.

title: Unique Paths III
hints: Find the start cell (value 1) and end cell (value 2); count all non-obstacle cells — every path must visit all of them.
DFS/backtracking from start: mark cells as visited, recurse in 4 directions, unmark on return.
A path is valid only when you reach the end cell AND have visited every non-obstacle cell exactly once.
approach: Scan the grid to find start, end, and count of cells to visit (non-obstacle). Run DFS from start, tracking visited cells. When reaching end with remaining count == 0, increment the answer. Backtrack by unmarking visited cells.
time_complexity_explanation: In the worst case, backtracking explores all possible paths in the grid.
space_complexity_explanation: Recursion stack depth is bounded by the number of cells.
key_insight: The constraint that every non-obstacle cell must be visited exactly once makes this a Hamiltonian path problem, solvable only by exhaustive backtracking.

title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock with Transaction Fee
hints: Track two states: cash (max profit when not holding stock) and hold (max profit when holding stock).
At each price: hold = max(hold, cash - price) and cash = max(cash, hold + price - fee).
The fee is subtracted once per completed transaction — apply it when selling.
approach: Maintain cash (profit without a stock) and hold (profit while holding a stock). For each price, update hold = max(hold, cash - price) and cash = max(cash, hold + price - fee). The fee is paid once per sell. Return cash at the end.
time_complexity_explanation: Single pass through the prices array.
space_complexity_explanation: Only two scalar DP variables are maintained.
key_insight: The fee can be modeled as subtracting from the sell price, reducing the problem to the same two-state DP as the unlimited transactions stock problem.

title: Longest Balanced Subarray I
hints: Map 0 -> -1 and 1 -> +1. A balanced subarray has sum 0, meaning prefix_sum[r] == prefix_sum[l-1].
Store the first index where each prefix sum is seen. When the same prefix sum reappears, update the maximum length.
Initialize the map with {0: -1} to handle subarrays starting from index 0.
approach: Convert 0s to -1s. Track running prefix sum. Use a hash map to record the first occurrence of each prefix sum. When a prefix sum repeats, the subarray between the two indices is balanced. Return the maximum such length.
time_complexity_explanation: Single pass through the string.
space_complexity_explanation: Hash map stores at most n+1 prefix sum entries.
key_insight: The classic 'equal zeros and ones' trick maps 0->-1 so a balanced subarray corresponds to a zero-sum subrange, detectable with a prefix sum hash map.

title: Longest Balanced Subarray II
hints: Consider the constraint that the subarray must have equal numbers of 0s and 1s AND every prefix must have at least as many 0s as 1s (like valid parentheses).
Use two passes: left-to-right tracking running balance, reset when balance < 0; right-to-left tracking running balance, reset when balance > 0.
The answer is the maximum window length found across both passes — combining both directions handles subarrays without the strict prefix condition.
approach: Perform two passes similar to the valid parentheses counting method. Left-to-right: increment for '0', decrement for '1'; when counts are equal update answer, reset when '1' count exceeds '0' count. Right-to-left: same logic reversed. Take the maximum across both passes.
time_complexity_explanation: Two linear passes over the string.
space_complexity_explanation: Only two counters are maintained per pass.
key_insight: Two opposite-direction passes (mirroring the bracket-counting technique) together cover all balanced substrings without needing extra space.

title: Shortest Unsorted Continuous Subarray
hints: Think about what the sorted version of the array looks like — find the leftmost and rightmost positions where the original array differs from its sorted version.
Alternatively, use a single pass: track the running maximum from left; whenever you see a value smaller than the running max, it must be included in the unsorted subarray.
Do a similar pass from right tracking the running minimum to find the left boundary of the subarray.
approach: Compare the array to its sorted version and find the leftmost and rightmost indices where they differ. The subarray between those indices (inclusive) is the shortest unsorted continuous subarray. Alternatively, scan left-to-right tracking the max and right-to-left tracking the min to find boundaries in O(n) time without extra space.
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting the array takes O(n log n); the two linear scans are O(n), dominated by the sort.
space_complexity_explanation: A copy of the array is created for sorting, requiring O(n) extra space.
key_insight: The shortest unsorted subarray is exactly the range where the original array disagrees with its sorted version; finding the first and last disagreement positions gives the boundaries directly.

title: Longest Balanced Substring I
hints: A balanced binary string has equal numbers of '0' and '1'. Map '0' to -1 and '1' to +1.
Use a prefix sum and a hash map recording the first index at which each prefix sum appears.
When a prefix sum is seen again, the subarray between the two indices is balanced — track the maximum such length.
approach: Scan the string while maintaining a running sum (+1 for '1', -1 for '0'). Record the first occurrence of each running sum in a hash map (initialized with {0: -1}). Each time a sum repeats, the balanced substring length is i - first_occurrence; update the maximum.
time_complexity_explanation: Single pass through the string with O(1) map operations per step.
space_complexity_explanation: Hash map stores at most n+1 distinct prefix sum values.
key_insight: The prefix-sum hash map technique reduces the balanced substring search to a repeated-sum lookup, solvable in a single linear scan.

title: Sequence Reconstruction
hints: Build a graph of ordering constraints: for each consecutive pair [a, b] in any sequence, a must come before b.
Topological sort (Kahn's algorithm) must produce exactly one valid order — at no point should the BFS queue have more than one node.
Also verify that every element in nums appears in at least one sequence and all sequence values are in [1, n].
approach: Build a directed graph from consecutive pairs in all sequences, deduplicating edges. Run Kahn's BFS topological sort. The sort is unique (nums is the only supersequence) iff the queue never contains more than one node simultaneously. Verify the resulting order matches nums.
time_complexity: O(V + E) where V = n and E = total pairs in sequences
time_complexity_explanation: Graph construction and Kahn's BFS each run in O(V + E).
space_complexity_explanation: Graph adjacency and indegree structures store all vertices and edges.
key_insight: nums is the unique shortest supersequence iff the topological sort at every step has exactly one choice — a queue size > 1 means ambiguity.

title: Path Sum III
hints: Use prefix sum along the root-to-node path. A path from some ancestor to the current node sums to target if prefix_sum[current] - prefix_sum[ancestor] == target.
Maintain a hash map of prefix sum frequencies seen on the current root-to-node path. At each node, look up (current_sum - target).
Increment the count in the map before recursing into children and decrement when backtracking to avoid cross-branch contamination.
approach: DFS through the tree, maintaining a running prefix sum and a hash map of prefix sum counts (initialized with {0: 1}). At each node, add prefix_sum_map[current_sum - target] to the answer, then recurse left and right. Decrement the map entry after recursing (backtracking).
solution_python:
class Solution:
    def pathSum(self, root: Optional[TreeNode], targetSum: int) -> int:
        from collections import defaultdict
        prefix = defaultdict(int)
        prefix[0] = 1
        self.ans = 0
        def dfs(node, curr):
            if not node: return
            curr += node.val
            self.ans += prefix[curr - targetSum]
            prefix[curr] += 1
            dfs(node.left, curr)
            dfs(node.right, curr)
            prefix[curr] -= 1
        dfs(root, 0)
        return self.ans
time_complexity_explanation: Each node is visited exactly once; hash map operations are O(1).
space_complexity_explanation: Hash map and recursion stack each use O(n) space in the worst case (skewed tree).
key_insight: Prefix sums along root-to-node paths reduce path-sum counting to a hash map lookup, avoiding the O(n²) brute-force approach.

title: Fruit Into Baskets
hints: The problem reduces to: find the longest subarray containing at most 2 distinct values.
Use a sliding window with a frequency map. Expand the right pointer; when distinct types exceed 2, shrink the left pointer.
When shrinking, decrement the frequency of fruits[left]; remove from map when count reaches 0.
approach: Sliding window with a frequency hash map. Expand right pointer unconditionally. When the map exceeds 2 distinct fruit types, advance the left pointer, decrementing counts and removing entries that reach 0. Track the maximum window size.
time_complexity_explanation: Each element is added and removed from the window at most once.
space_complexity_explanation: The frequency map holds at most 3 entries at any time.
key_insight: Framing as 'longest subarray with at most k distinct elements' (k=2) reveals the classic sliding window pattern.

title: Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array II
hints: Use a write pointer k. An element nums[i] is accepted if k < 2 or nums[k-2] != nums[i].
Because the array is sorted, checking nums[k-2] is enough to enforce the 'at most 2 occurrences' rule.
Advance k only when placing an accepted element; always advance i.
approach: Maintain write pointer k (initially 0). For each element nums[i]: if k < 2 or nums[k-2] != nums[i], write nums[i] to nums[k] and increment k. Return k as the new length. This allows at most 2 of each value.
time_complexity_explanation: Single pass through the array.
space_complexity_explanation: In-place modification with only one extra pointer.
key_insight: Comparing with nums[k-2] (not nums[k-1]) is the minimal change from the 'at most 1' version that generalizes to any 'at most m' limit.

title: Basic Calculator II
hints: Process the expression character by character, building the current number. When you hit an operator or end, apply the previous operator.
For '+'/'-' push the (signed) number onto the stack; for '*'/'/' pop the top, compute, and push the result — this handles precedence.
Final answer is the sum of everything remaining on the stack.
approach: Iterate through the string tracking the current number and the previous operator. On encountering an operator or end of string: if previous op is + or -, push the signed number; if * or /, pop, compute, push result. Sum the stack for the final answer.
time_complexity_explanation: Single pass over the expression string.
space_complexity_explanation: Stack can hold at most O(n) numbers in the worst case (all additions).
key_insight: Immediately resolving * and / when encountered (by popping and pushing) naturally enforces operator precedence without needing parenthesis handling.

title: Sudoku Solver
hints: For each empty cell try digits '1'-'9'. Check validity against the cell's row, column, and 3x3 box.
Recurse to the next empty cell; if placement leads to a dead end, backtrack by clearing the cell.
Pre-build row, col, and box constraint sets from the initial board to make validity checks O(1).
approach: Collect all empty cells. Try placing '1'-'9' in each; validate using pre-built row/col/box sets. On valid placement, recurse to the next empty cell. If recursion succeeds return True; otherwise remove the digit and try the next. The board is solved in-place.
time_complexity: O(9^m) where m is the number of empty cells
time_complexity_explanation: In the worst case each empty cell tries all 9 digits, but constraint propagation prunes the search drastically in practice.
space_complexity_explanation: Recursion depth equals the number of empty cells m; constraint sets use O(81) = O(1) extra space.
key_insight: Constraint sets (row/col/box) reduce each validity check to O(1), making the backtracking practical for a 9x9 board.

title: Binary Number with Alternating Bits
hints: XOR n with n>>1: if the result is all 1s (i.e., result & (result+1) == 0), the bits alternate.
Alternatively, check consecutive bits by repeatedly stripping the last two bits and verifying they differ.
Numbers with alternating bits: 1,2,5,10,21,42... — notice the pattern in binary.
approach: XOR n with n>>1. If bits alternate, this produces a number of all 1s. Check if (n ^ (n>>1)) & ((n ^ (n>>1)) + 1) == 0.
time_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of bitwise operations are performed.
space_complexity_explanation: No extra space used beyond a couple of variables.
key_insight: XOR with right-shift produces all 1s only when bits alternate; checking all-1s is equivalent to m & (m+1) == 0.

title: Logger Rate Limiter
hints: Use a hash map from message string to the last timestamp it was printed.
For each call, check if the message is new or if at least 10 seconds have elapsed since its last print; if so, allow and update the map.
This is a classic sliding-window / rate-limit pattern — only the last allowed timestamp needs to be stored, not the full history.
approach: Maintain a dictionary mapping each message to the last timestamp it was successfully printed. On each shouldPrintMessage call, if the message is unseen or current_timestamp - stored_timestamp >= 10, update the map and return True; otherwise return False.
time_complexity_explanation: Each call is a single hash map lookup and possible update, both O(1).
space_complexity_explanation: The map stores at most one entry per unique message seen, so O(n) where n is the number of distinct messages.
key_insight: Storing only the last-allowed timestamp per message is sufficient; no queue or sliding window history is needed.

title: Number of Ways to Arrive at Destination
hints: Run Dijkstra from node 0, but also maintain a ways[] array counting the number of shortest paths to each node.
When relaxing an edge: if the new distance is strictly shorter, update dist and reset ways; if equal to the current shortest, add the source node's ways count.
Return ways[n-1] modulo 1e9+7.
approach: Use Dijkstra's algorithm with a min-heap. Alongside dist[], maintain ways[] initialised to 0 except ways[0]=1. When popping node u with distance d, for each neighbour v: if d + w < dist[v], update dist[v] and set ways[v] = ways[u]; if d + w == dist[v], add ways[u] to ways[v]. Return ways[n-1] mod (1e9+7).
solution_python:
import heapq
from typing import List

class Solution:
    def countPaths(self, n: int, roads: List[List[int]]) -> int:
        MOD = 10**9 + 7
        graph = [[] for _ in range(n)]
        for u, v, w in roads:
            graph[u].append((v, w))
            graph[v].append((u, w))
        dist = [float('inf')] * n
        ways = [0] * n
        dist[0] = 0
        ways[0] = 1
        heap = [(0, 0)]
        while heap:
            d, u = heapq.heappop(heap)
            if d > dist[u]:
                continue
            for v, w in graph[u]:
                nd = d + w
                if nd < dist[v]:
                    dist[v] = nd
                    ways[v] = ways[u]
                    heapq.heappush(heap, (nd, v))
                elif nd == dist[v]:
                    ways[v] = (ways[v] + ways[u]) % MOD
        return ways[n - 1]
time_complexity_explanation: Standard Dijkstra complexity: each edge is relaxed once and each heap operation is O(log V).
space_complexity_explanation: Adjacency list stores all edges; dist and ways arrays are O(V); heap holds at most O(E) entries.
key_insight: Dijkstra naturally tracks shortest distances; augmenting it with a ways[] counter that resets on improvement and accumulates on equality counts all shortest paths in one pass.

title: Sort an Array
hints: Think about which O(n log n) sorting algorithm can be implemented without using built-in sort.
Merge sort divides the array in half recursively, sorts each half, then merges the two sorted halves.
For the merge step, use a temporary array; copy elements back once merged.
approach: Implement merge sort: recursively split the array into two halves until size 1, then merge sorted halves back together in O(n) time. The merge step uses a two-pointer technique to interleave elements from both halves in sorted order. This achieves O(n log n) in all cases with O(n) auxiliary space.
time_complexity_explanation: Merge sort divides the array log n times and each level does O(n) work during the merge step.
space_complexity_explanation: Auxiliary arrays created during merging total O(n) space across all levels of recursion.
key_insight: Merge sort is the go-to when a stable O(n log n) sort is needed; the divide-and-conquer structure guarantees worst-case performance unlike quicksort.

title: Second Highest Salary
hints: Use LIMIT and OFFSET to skip the highest salary and grab the next one.
Wrap the query in a subquery or use IFNULL/COALESCE to return NULL when no second salary exists.
Use DISTINCT to avoid counting duplicate top salaries as distinct ranks.
approach: Select the maximum salary that is strictly less than the overall maximum, or equivalently use LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1 on a DESC-ordered DISTINCT salary list. Wrapping in IFNULL/COALESCE ensures NULL is returned when fewer than two distinct salaries exist.
solution_python:
-- SQL Solution
SELECT IFNULL(
    (SELECT DISTINCT salary
     FROM Employee
     ORDER BY salary DESC
     LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1),
    NULL
) AS SecondHighestSalary;
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting all employee salaries to find the second distinct maximum takes O(n log n).
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant amount of extra space is needed beyond the input table.
key_insight: Using DISTINCT before ordering ensures duplicate top salaries don't masquerade as the second-highest; IFNULL handles the edge case of fewer than two distinct values.

title: The Earliest Moment When Everyone Become Friends
hints: Sort all friendship logs by timestamp so you can process them in chronological order.
Use Union-Find (DSU) to merge groups of friends; track the number of connected components.
When the number of components drops to 1, the current timestamp is the answer.
approach: Sort the logs by timestamp. Use a Union-Find structure initialised with n components (one per person). For each log entry in order, union the two people; if they were in different components, decrement the component count. The first timestamp at which the component count reaches 1 is the answer. If it never reaches 1, return -1.
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting the m logs dominates; each union/find operation is nearly O(1) amortised with path compression and union by rank.
space_complexity_explanation: Union-Find arrays (parent and rank) each have n entries.
key_insight: Union-Find is ideal here because we only need to track connected components and detect when they merge into one; sorting logs first ensures we process friendships in the correct chronological order.

title: Set Mismatch
hints: Count the frequency of each number; the one with frequency 2 is the duplicate.
The missing number is the one with frequency 0, or equivalently compute expected sum minus actual sum after finding the duplicate.
Alternatively, use the XOR trick: XOR all indices and all values to isolate the duplicate and missing.
approach: Use a frequency count array of size n+1. Iterate over nums, incrementing counts. The number whose count is 2 is the duplicate; the number whose count is 0 is the missing. Alternatively, compute the difference between the expected sum 1..n and the actual sum; combined with the duplicate found from the count, derive both answers.
time_complexity_explanation: Two linear passes over the input and count array, each O(n).
space_complexity_explanation: A count array of size n+1 is allocated to track frequencies.
key_insight: A simple frequency array makes it trivial to spot both the duplicate (count 2) and the missing (count 0) in two passes, avoiding the complexity of sorting.

title: Longest Duplicate Substring
hints: Binary search on the answer: if a duplicate substring of length k exists, one of length k-1 also exists.
Use Rabin-Karp rolling hash to check for duplicate substrings of a given length in O(n) time.
Choose a large prime modulus to minimise hash collisions; verify candidate matches when a collision is found.
approach: Binary search on the length L of the duplicate substring. For each candidate L, use a rolling hash (Rabin-Karp) to compute hashes of all substrings of length L in O(n) time and store them in a set. If any hash appears twice, a duplicate of length L exists. Return the longest L for which a duplicate is found. Use a large modulus to keep collision probability low.
time_complexity_explanation: Binary search runs O(log n) iterations; each rolling hash check scans O(n) characters, giving O(n log n) overall.
space_complexity_explanation: The hash set stores up to O(n) entries for each candidate length check.
key_insight: Combining binary search on length with Rabin-Karp rolling hash converts an O(n^2) brute-force into O(n log n); the monotone property (if length k works, k-1 works too) makes binary search valid.

title: Array Prototype Last
hints: Extend the Array prototype by adding a method called 'last'.
Return the last element using index length-1, or -1 if the array is empty.
No loops or recursion needed — a single conditional expression suffices.
approach: Add a 'last' method to Array.prototype. The method checks whether the array is empty; if so it returns -1, otherwise it returns the element at index this.length - 1.
time_complexity_explanation: Accessing the last element of an array by index is a constant-time operation.
space_complexity_explanation: No additional data structures are allocated.
key_insight: Prototype extension in JavaScript lets you add utility methods to built-in types; the empty-array guard is the only edge case.

title: Rearrange Array Elements by Sign
hints: The result array alternates: positive at even indices, negative at odd indices.
Maintain two separate pointers/indices — one for the next positive slot (even) and one for the next negative slot (odd).
Iterate through nums once and place each element directly into its correct position in the output array.
approach: Create an output array of the same size. Keep two indices: posIdx starting at 0 (even positions) and negIdx starting at 1 (odd positions). Iterate through nums; when a positive number is encountered place it at posIdx and advance posIdx by 2; when negative, place at negIdx and advance negIdx by 2. This fills the output in a single pass.
time_complexity_explanation: A single pass through the input array of n elements fills the output array.
space_complexity_explanation: An output array of the same size n is allocated.
key_insight: Because equal numbers of positives and negatives are guaranteed, two independent pointers advancing by 2 will exactly fill all even/odd slots without any collision.

title: Backspace String Compare
hints: Simulate the backspace with a stack: push characters, pop on '#'.
Compare the resulting stacks (or rebuilt strings) for equality.
For O(1) space, traverse both strings from the end using two pointers and a skip counter to handle backspaces without building new strings.
approach: Two approaches: (1) Stack simulation — iterate each string and push characters onto a stack, popping on '#'. Compare final stacks. O(n) time and space. (2) Two-pointer from the end — traverse both strings right-to-left maintaining a skip count that increments on '#' and causes characters to be skipped. Compare character by character. O(n) time, O(1) space.
time_complexity_explanation: Each character in both strings is processed exactly once by the stack simulation.
space_complexity_explanation: In the worst case the stacks hold all characters from both strings.
key_insight: A stack perfectly models backspace behavior; the two-pointer from-the-end approach upgrades space to O(1) by lazily consuming backspaces as a skip counter.

title: Find Missing and Repeated Values
hints: Flatten the 2D grid into a 1D list of numbers to simplify processing.
Count frequencies; the number appearing twice is the repeated value.
The missing number is in range 1..n*n and has frequency 0.
approach: Flatten the n x n grid to a list of all values. Build a frequency counter. Iterate from 1 to n*n: the value with count 2 is the repeated number, and the value with count 0 is the missing number. Return them as [repeated, missing].
time_complexity_explanation: Every cell in the n x n grid is visited once, and the count array is scanned once over n^2 entries.
space_complexity_explanation: A count array of size n^2 + 1 is allocated to store frequencies.
key_insight: The problem is structurally identical to Set Mismatch on a 2D grid; flattening and frequency counting reduces it to the same O(n^2) linear scan.

title: Number of Digit One
hints: Count the contribution of digit '1' at each position (ones, tens, hundreds, etc.) separately.
For a digit position with factor f, compute: higher = n // (f * 10), current = (n // f) % 10, lower = n % f.
If current == 0: count += higher * f; if current == 1: count += higher * f + lower + 1; if current > 1: count += (higher + 1) * f.
approach: Iterate over each digit position (factor = 1, 10, 100, ...). For each position compute: higher digits (n // (factor*10)), current digit ((n // factor) % 10), and lower digits (n % factor). The number of 1s contributed by this position follows three cases based on whether the current digit is 0, 1, or greater than 1. Sum contributions across all positions.
time_complexity_explanation: The loop iterates once per digit of n, which is O(log n) iterations.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of variables are used regardless of input size.
key_insight: Counting digit '1' position by position and using the three-case formula (current < 1, == 1, > 1) avoids brute-force enumeration entirely, reducing complexity from O(n log n) to O(log n).

title: Divide Array in Sets of K Consecutive Numbers
hints: Sort the array and use a frequency map to track remaining counts.
Always try to form a group starting from the smallest available number.
If the smallest available number cannot start a full consecutive group of size k, return false immediately.
approach: Sort the array and build a frequency map of element counts. Iterate through sorted unique values; for each value that still has remaining count, attempt to consume k consecutive numbers starting from it. If any number in the sequence is missing or exhausted, return false. If all numbers are consumed into valid groups, return true.
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting takes O(n log n) and iterating over the frequency map with k-size windows is O(n) total.
space_complexity_explanation: The frequency map stores at most n entries.
key_insight: Greedily consuming from the smallest unplaced number ensures correctness — if the smallest element cannot start a valid group, it can never be placed in any other group.

title: Swap Adjacent in LR String
hints: Ignoring X characters, the relative order of L and R must be identical in both strings.
L can only move left (swaps with X to its left), R can only move right (swaps with X to its right).
Use two pointers: for each matching L/R pair, check that its position satisfies the movement constraints.
approach: Filter out X from both strings and verify that the remaining L/R characters appear in the same order — if not, return false. Then use two pointers to walk through both strings simultaneously, skipping X characters. For each aligned non-X pair, verify: an L in start must be at an index >= the corresponding L in end (L moves left), and an R in start must be at an index <= the corresponding R in end (R moves right).
solution_python:
class Solution:
    def canTransform(self, start: str, end: str) -> bool:
        if start.replace('X', '') != end.replace('X', ''):
            return False
        i, j = 0, 0
        n = len(start)
        while i < n and j < n:
            while i < n and start[i] == 'X': i += 1
            while j < n and end[j] == 'X': j += 1
            if i == n and j == n: return True
            if i == n or j == n: return False
            if start[i] != end[j]: return False
            if start[i] == 'L' and i < j: return False
            if start[i] == 'R' and i > j: return False
            i += 1
            j += 1
        return True
time_complexity_explanation: Both pointers traverse the string at most once, and the replace operation is O(n).
space_complexity_explanation: The replace operation creates new strings of length up to n.
key_insight: The key observation is that X's are transparent — only the relative ordering of L/R matters, and the direction each character can move (L only left, R only right) gives a clean positional constraint to check.

title: N-th Tribonacci Number
hints: The Tribonacci sequence is defined as T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + T(n-3) with T(0)=0, T(1)=1, T(2)=1.
You only need the last three values at any point, so O(1) space is achievable.
Iterate from index 3 up to n, updating a rolling triple of values.
approach: Use iterative dynamic programming with O(1) space by maintaining only the last three Tribonacci numbers. Handle base cases for n=0,1,2 directly, then iterate from 3 to n updating a rolling triple (a, b, c) at each step by computing a+b+c and shifting the window forward.
time_complexity_explanation: A single loop iterates n-2 times to compute the nth Tribonacci number.
space_complexity_explanation: Only three variables are maintained regardless of n.
key_insight: Like Fibonacci but summing three prior terms; the rolling-triple approach eliminates the need for an array, achieving O(1) space while maintaining O(n) time.

title: Sum of Subarray Minimums
hints: For each element, determine how many subarrays have it as the minimum.
Use a monotonic stack to find the nearest smaller element to the left and right for each index.
The contribution of arr[i] is arr[i] * left_distance * right_distance, where distances come from the next-smaller boundaries.
approach: For each element arr[i], compute left[i] = number of subarrays ending at i where arr[i] is the minimum, and right[i] = number of subarrays starting at i where arr[i] is the minimum. Use two monotonic stack passes: one leftward to find the previous smaller element, one rightward to find the next smaller or equal element (to avoid double-counting duplicates). The answer is the sum of arr[i] * left[i] * right[i] modulo 1e9+7.
time_complexity_explanation: Each element is pushed and popped from the monotonic stack at most once in each of the two passes.
space_complexity_explanation: The left, right, and stack arrays each hold at most n elements.
key_insight: Instead of enumerating all O(n^2) subarrays, count the contribution of each element as the minimum: use monotonic stacks to find its dominance boundaries in O(n) total.

title: Binary Gap
hints: Convert the number to binary and scan for positions of 1-bits.
Track the index of the last seen 1-bit as you iterate.
The answer is the maximum gap between any two consecutive 1-bits.
approach: Iterate through the bits of n from LSB to MSB using bit shifting. Track the position of the last seen '1' bit. Whenever a new '1' bit is found, compute the gap from the previous '1' position and update the maximum. Return the maximum gap found.
time_complexity_explanation: The loop runs once per bit in the binary representation of n, which is O(log n) iterations.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of variables are used.
key_insight: You only need to remember the position of the last '1' bit seen — no need to store the full binary string — making this a clean O(log n) time, O(1) space scan.

title: Check If a String Contains All Binary Codes of Size K
hints: There are exactly 2^k distinct binary codes of length k.
Use a sliding window of size k over the string and collect all unique substrings.
If the number of unique substrings equals 2^k, all codes are present.
approach: Slide a window of size k across the string s, inserting each k-length substring into a set. After processing all substrings, check if the set size equals 2^k. An optimized approach uses rolling hash (treating each window as a binary number) to avoid re-hashing the full substring each step.
time_complexity_explanation: Each of the O(n) sliding window positions produces a k-length substring to hash and insert into the set.
space_complexity_explanation: The set stores up to 2^k strings each of length k.
key_insight: The total number of distinct binary codes of length k is exactly 2^k, so we just need to check that a sliding window collects that many unique substrings — early termination keeps it efficient in practice.

title: Sum of Root To Leaf Binary Numbers
hints: Perform a DFS and maintain the current binary number formed from root to the current node.
At each node, shift the accumulated value left by 1 and OR in the current node's value.
When a leaf is reached, add the accumulated value to the total sum.
approach: Use a recursive DFS traversal. At each node, compute the running binary number by shifting the parent's value left by 1 and adding the current node's value (0 or 1). When a leaf node is reached (no children), add the accumulated value to the result. Return the sum of all leaf path values.
time_complexity_explanation: Every node is visited exactly once in the DFS traversal.
space_complexity_explanation: The recursion stack depth equals the height h of the tree, which is O(log n) for balanced trees and O(n) worst case.
key_insight: Passing the running binary number down the recursion via bit-shift-and-OR elegantly accumulates the root-to-leaf path value without needing a separate path list.

title: Sort Integers by The Number of 1 Bits
hints: Count the number of 1-bits (popcount) in each integer.
Sort by popcount as primary key and by value as secondary key.
Python's bin() and count('1'), or bin.bit_count() in Python 3.10+, make popcount easy.
approach: Sort the array using a custom comparator with two keys: first the popcount (number of set bits), then the value itself for tie-breaking. In Python this is a one-liner using sorted() with a lambda key tuple. In JavaScript, sort with a comparator that first compares bit counts and falls back to value comparison.
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting n elements dominates at O(n log n); each comparison computes popcount in O(log max_val) time.
space_complexity_explanation: Python's sorted() and JS sort use O(n) auxiliary space for the output or temporary keys.
key_insight: Using a tuple key (popcount, value) in Python's stable sort elegantly handles both the primary and secondary sorting criteria in a single expression.

title: Number of Steps to Reduce a Number in Binary Representation to One
hints: Process the binary string from the rightmost bit toward the left.
If the current last bit is 0, remove it (right shift / divide by 2) — that's 1 step.
If the current last bit is 1, adding 1 may cause a carry chain; simulate carry propagation.
approach: Work directly on the binary string from right to left. Maintain a carry variable. For each bit (right to left, skipping the leading '1'): if bit+carry is even, it costs 1 step (right shift); if bit+carry is odd, it costs 2 steps (add 1 then right shift) and sets carry=1. Add remaining carry steps. This avoids converting to an integer which could overflow for large inputs.
time_complexity_explanation: A single right-to-left pass over the n-character binary string.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of variables (steps, carry, bit) are used.
key_insight: Simulating carry propagation on the binary string directly avoids integer overflow issues for very long inputs and keeps the solution O(n) with O(1) extra space.

title: Concatenation of Consecutive Binary Numbers
hints: The number of bits needed to represent i is floor(log2(i)) + 1.
To append i to the running number, shift the current result left by the bit-length of i, then OR in i.
The bit-length of i only increases when i is a power of 2 (i.e., i & (i-1) == 0).
approach: Iterate i from 1 to n. For each i, determine its bit length (which only increases at powers of 2, detectable via i & (i-1) == 0). Shift the running result left by that bit length and OR in i. Take modulo 1e9+7 at each step to prevent overflow. This achieves O(n) time with O(1) space.
time_complexity_explanation: A single loop from 1 to n with O(1) work per iteration.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of variables are maintained regardless of n.
key_insight: Detecting bit-length increases in O(1) using the power-of-two check (i & (i-1) == 0) avoids a log computation each iteration, keeping the whole solution clean at O(n) time and O(1) space.

title: Number of Substrings Containing All Three Characters
hints: For every right pointer position, find the leftmost left such that s[left..right] contains all three characters.
Any substring ending at right that starts at or before that left index is also valid.
Track the last seen index of each character 'a', 'b', 'c' to compute the boundary in O(1).
approach: Use a sliding window by tracking the last occurrence of each character 'a', 'b', 'c'. For each right index, the earliest valid left boundary is min(last['a'], last['b'], last['c']) + 1. All substrings from indices 0..boundary are valid (there are boundary + 1 of them). Accumulate this count across all right positions.
time_complexity_explanation: A single left-to-right pass over the string of length n.
space_complexity_explanation: Only three variables track the last seen positions of 'a', 'b', 'c'.
key_insight: Tracking the last occurrence of each character lets you count valid substrings ending at the current position in O(1) — the number of valid left endpoints equals min(last_a, last_b, last_c) + 1.

title: Partitioning Into Minimum Number Of Deci-Binary Numbers
hints: A deci-binary number only has digits 0 and 1.
Each deci-binary number can contribute at most 1 to any digit position.
To build a digit d at some position, you need at least d deci-binary numbers.
approach: Since each deci-binary number contributes at most 1 to each digit position, to reconstruct a digit of value d you need at least d numbers. Therefore the minimum count is determined by the maximum digit in the string. The answer is simply the maximum digit character in n.
time_complexity_explanation: Scanning all digits of the string once to find the maximum.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a single variable is needed to track the maximum digit.
key_insight: Each deci-binary number contributes at most 1 per digit position, so the hardest digit to satisfy is the maximum digit — that digit alone dictates how many numbers are needed.

title: Find Kth Bit in Nth Binary String
hints: The nth string has length 2^n - 1. The middle element is always '1'.
If k is in the second half, it mirrors a position in the first half but flipped.
Recurse: map k in the second half to its mirror in the first half via k' = 2^n - k, then flip the result.
approach: Use recursion. The nth binary string S_n has length L = 2^n - 1. The middle index is (L+1)/2. If k equals the middle, return '1'. If k < middle, recurse with find(n-1, k). Otherwise (k > middle), recurse with find(n-1, L+1-k) and flip the returned bit. Base case: n=1 returns '0'.
time_complexity_explanation: Each recursive call decrements n by 1, so there are at most n levels of recursion.
space_complexity_explanation: The recursion stack depth is at most n frames.
key_insight: The second half of S_n is the reverse-and-complement of S_{n-1}, so any query in the second half can be mapped to an equivalent query in the first half via index mirroring plus a bit flip.

title: Minimum Number of Flips to Make the Binary String Alternating
hints: An alternating binary string of length n is either '010101...' or '101010...'.
Type-1 operations (cyclic rotation) correspond to a sliding window of size n over s+s.
Count mismatches against both target patterns within the window; the answer is min of the two mismatch counts.
approach: Double the string (s = s + s) to simulate cyclic rotations. Build two target alternating strings of the doubled length. Use a sliding window of size n over the doubled string; as you slide, maintain the number of mismatches with each target using O(1) updates. The answer is the minimum mismatch count seen across all windows.
solution_python:
class Solution:
    def minFlips(self, s: str) -> int:
        n = len(s)
        s2 = s + s
        t1 = ''.join('0' if i % 2 == 0 else '1' for i in range(2 * n))
        t2 = ''.join('1' if i % 2 == 0 else '0' for i in range(2 * n))
        diff1 = sum(s2[i] != t1[i] for i in range(n))
        diff2 = sum(s2[i] != t2[i] for i in range(n))
        ans = min(diff1, diff2)
        for i in range(n, 2 * n):
            if s2[i] != t1[i]: diff1 += 1
            if s2[i] != t2[i]: diff2 += 1
            if s2[i - n] != t1[i - n]: diff1 -= 1
            if s2[i - n] != t2[i - n]: diff2 -= 1
            ans = min(ans, diff1, diff2)
        return ans
time_complexity_explanation: One O(n) pass to initialize the window and one O(n) pass to slide it, both over the doubled string.
space_complexity_explanation: The doubled string and two target strings each require O(n) space.
key_insight: Doubling the string converts cyclic rotations into a standard sliding window problem; tracking mismatches incrementally as the window slides gives O(n) total work.

title: Find All Possible Stable Binary Arrays I
hints: Define dp[i][j][last] = number of stable arrays using i zeros and j ones, ending with 'last'.
A transition is valid only if it doesn't create a run of more than limit identical values.
Use prefix sums over dp states to efficiently subtract out invalid transitions.
approach: Use 3D DP: dp[i][j][k] = number of stable arrays with i zeros, j ones, ending with digit k (0 or 1). For each state, sum over all valid previous run lengths up to limit. Use prefix sums to compute range sums efficiently, achieving O(zero * one * limit) overall. Take results modulo 10^9 + 7.
time_complexity_explanation: There are O(zero * one) distinct (z, o, last) states and each transition iterates up to limit times.
space_complexity_explanation: The memoization table stores O(zero * one * 2) entries.
key_insight: Modeling runs of same-digit values as the DP dimension keeps transitions straightforward; prefix sums can further reduce per-transition cost to O(1) for larger inputs.

title: Maximum Profit in Job Scheduling
hints: Sort jobs by end time so you can build up the DP from left to right.
For each job, binary search for the latest job that ends at or before this job's start time.
dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], profit[i] + dp[last_non_overlapping_index]).
approach: Sort all jobs by end time. Define dp[i] as the maximum profit considering the first i jobs. For job i, use binary search to find the latest job j whose end time <= start time of job i. Then dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[j] + profit[i]). The base case dp[0] = 0 represents taking no jobs.
solution_python:
from bisect import bisect_right
class Solution:
    def jobScheduling(self, startTime, endTime, profit):
        jobs = sorted(zip(endTime, startTime, profit))
        end_times = [0]
        dp = [0]
        for end, start, p in jobs:
            # find latest job ending <= start
            idx = bisect_right(end_times, start) - 1
            best = dp[idx] + p
            if best > dp[-1]:
                dp.append(best)
                end_times.append(end)
            else:
                dp.append(dp[-1])
                end_times.append(end)
        return dp[-1]
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting takes O(n log n) and each of the n jobs does one O(log n) binary search.
space_complexity_explanation: The dp array and end_times array each store at most n+1 entries.
key_insight: Sorting by end time enables a clean left-to-right DP where binary search on end times pinpoints the best compatible previous state in O(log n), making the overall approach O(n log n).

title: Duplicate Emails
hints: Group rows by the email column and count occurrences.
Filter groups where the count is greater than 1.
Use HAVING to filter after GROUP BY.
approach: Group the Person table by email and count occurrences. Use HAVING COUNT(*) > 1 to retain only emails that appear more than once. SELECT that email column as the result.
solution_python:
SELECT email AS Email
FROM Person
GROUP BY email
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: The database scans all n rows to build the group aggregates.
space_complexity_explanation: Intermediate grouping may require O(n) space for the hash table of email counts.
key_insight: GROUP BY + HAVING is the idiomatic SQL pattern for finding duplicates; the HAVING clause filters aggregated groups, unlike WHERE which filters individual rows.

title: Complement of Base 10 Integer
hints: To flip all bits of n, XOR with a mask of all 1s of the same bit-length.
The mask is 2^(bit_length) - 1, where bit_length = floor(log2(n)) + 1.
Special case: n = 0 has no set bits; its complement is 1.
approach: Find the number of bits in n using bit_length (or log2). Construct a mask with all those bits set to 1 (mask = 2^bit_length - 1). XOR n with the mask to flip all bits. Handle n=0 as a special case returning 1.
time_complexity_explanation: Computing the bit length requires O(log n) iterations (or O(1) with built-in bit_length).
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of integer variables are used.
key_insight: XORing with an all-ones mask of the same bit-width flips every bit cleanly without affecting higher bits, making this a one-liner once you compute the right mask.

title: Maximize Spanning Tree Stability with Upgrades
hints: Binary search on the answer: for a given stability threshold t, check if a spanning tree with minimum edge weight >= t is achievable after at most k upgrades.
Sort edges by weight descending and greedily include them; edges below the threshold can be upgraded if k allows.
Use Union-Find to efficiently check connectivity while accounting for upgraded edges.
approach: Binary search on the answer (minimum edge weight in the spanning tree). For a candidate answer t, sort edges by weight descending and use a greedy approach with Union-Find: include edges with weight >= t directly; for edges with weight < t, count how many upgrades would be needed. If the total needed upgrades <= k and the graph becomes connected, t is feasible. Return the maximum feasible t.
time_complexity_explanation: Binary search over O(E) distinct weights gives O(log E) iterations, each running a O(E log E) Kruskal-like pass, for O(E log E log E) total.
space_complexity_explanation: Union-Find and edge storage require O(V + E) space.
key_insight: Binary searching on the answer threshold converts a maximization problem into a series of feasibility checks, each solvable greedily with Union-Find by prioritizing high-weight edges and spending upgrade budget only when needed.

title: Minimum Number of Seconds to Make Mountain Height Zero
hints: Binary search on the total time T.
For a given T, compute how much height each worker can reduce and check if the total >= mountainHeight.
Worker i takes i*(i+1)/2 seconds to reduce height by i; use this to find the max reduction in time T.
approach: Binary search on the answer T (total seconds). For each worker with speed workerTimes[i], the time to reduce height by h is workerTimes[i] * h*(h+1)/2. Given T seconds, binary search or use the quadratic formula to find the maximum h each worker can achieve. Sum up reductions across all workers; if >= mountainHeight, T is feasible.
time_complexity_explanation: Binary search runs O(log(max_T)) iterations, each doing O(n) work across all workers.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant amount of extra space is used beyond the input array.
key_insight: Binary searching on time T with an O(1) per-worker feasibility check (via the closed-form quadratic inverse) turns an otherwise complex scheduling problem into a clean O(n log T) solution.

title: The k-th Lexicographical String of All Happy Strings of Length n
hints: A happy string uses only a, b, c and no two adjacent characters are the same.
Generate all happy strings of length n in lexicographical order using backtracking.
At each position, try a, b, c in order, skipping any character equal to the previous character.
approach: Use backtracking to generate all happy strings of length n in lexicographical order. At each step, try appending a, b, or c, skipping any character equal to the last character added. Once a string of length n is formed, add it to the result list. Return the k-th string (1-indexed) if it exists, otherwise return empty string.
time_complexity_explanation: There are 3 * 2^(n-1) happy strings of length n, and each takes O(n) to construct.
space_complexity_explanation: We store all happy strings, each of length n, in the result list.
key_insight: After choosing the first character (3 options), each subsequent character has exactly 2 choices (any of a/b/c except the previous), giving a predictable tree structure.

title: Get Biggest Three Rhombus Sums in a Grid
hints: A rhombus of size 0 is just a single cell. For size > 0, sum the four diagonal borders.
Use prefix sums along diagonals (top-left-to-bottom-right and top-right-to-bottom-left) to compute rhombus border sums in O(1).
Collect all rhombus sums, keep track of the top 3 distinct values using a sorted set.
approach: Precompute prefix sums along both diagonals. For each center cell and each possible rhombus size, compute the border sum using the diagonal prefix sums in O(1). Collect all rhombus sums (including size-0 single cells) and maintain the top 3 distinct values. Return them sorted in descending order.
solution_python:
class Solution:
    def getBiggestThree(self, grid: List[List[int]]) -> List[int]:
        m, n = len(grid), len(grid[0])
        # diagonal prefix sums: diag1[i][j] = top-left to bottom-right, diag2[i][j] = top-right to bottom-left
        diag1 = [[0] * (n + 2) for _ in range(m + 2)]
        diag2 = [[0] * (n + 2) for _ in range(m + 2)]
        for i in range(1, m + 1):
            for j in range(1, n + 1):
