                diag1[i][j] = diag1[i-1][j-1] + grid[i-1][j-1]
                diag2[i][j] = diag2[i-1][j+1] + grid[i-1][j-1]
        
        def rhombus_sum(r, c, k):
            if k == 0:
                return grid[r][c]
            r1, c1 = r - k, c
            r2, c2 = r, c + k
            r3, c3 = r + k, c
            r4, c4 = r, c - k
            ri, ci = r1 + 1, c1 + 1
            # top to right
            s1 = diag1[r2+1][c2+1] - diag1[ri][ci]
            # right to bottom
            s2 = diag2[r3+1][c3+1] - diag2[r2+1][c2+1]
            # bottom to left
            s3 = diag1[r3+1][c3+1] - diag1[r4][c4]
            # left to top
            s4 = diag2[r4+1][c4+1] - diag2[ri][ci]
            return s1 + s2 + s3 + s4 - grid[r1][c1] - grid[r2][c2] - grid[r3][c3] - grid[r4][c4]
        
        top3 = sorted(set())
        result = []
        import heapq
        seen = set()
        
        candidates = []
        for i in range(m):
            for j in range(n):
                max_k = min(i, j, m-1-i, n-1-j)
                for k in range(max_k + 1):
                    s = rhombus_sum(i, j, k)
                    candidates.append(s)
        
        distinct = sorted(set(candidates), reverse=True)
        return distinct[:3]
time_complexity_explanation: For each of the m*n cells we try up to min(m,n)/2 rhombus sizes, each computed in O(1) with prefix sums.
space_complexity_explanation: Two diagonal prefix sum matrices of size m*n are maintained.
key_insight: Precomputing diagonal prefix sums reduces each rhombus border sum query from O(n) to O(1), making the overall algorithm feasible.

title: Largest Submatrix With Rearrangements
hints: For each cell, compute the consecutive 1s going upward (column height ending at this row).
For each row, sort the column heights in descending order.
After sorting, the largest rectangle using j columns has height heights[j-1] and width j.
approach: First, transform the matrix into a height matrix where each cell stores the number of consecutive 1s ending at that row in its column. For each row, sort the heights in descending order. Then iterate through the sorted heights: for j columns (1-indexed), the rectangle area is heights[j-1] * j. Track the maximum area found.
time_complexity_explanation: Building heights is O(m*n) and sorting each row is O(n log n), done m times.
space_complexity_explanation: The matrix is modified in-place and sorting uses O(n) extra space per row.
key_insight: By sorting column heights per row, you can greedily select the widest possible submatrix for each height level, since columns can be freely rearranged.

title: Design Search Autocomplete System
hints: Use a Trie to store all sentences and their frequencies.
For each input character, traverse the Trie to the node matching the current prefix, then collect and rank all completions from that subtree.
When # is typed, insert the completed sentence into the Trie with incremented frequency.
approach: Build a Trie where each node stores sentence-frequency pairs. On each input() call, if the character is not #, append it to the current prefix and traverse the Trie to find the matching node, then do a DFS to collect all sentences in that subtree, returning the top 3 by frequency (ties broken lexicographically). If # is typed, add the current sentence to the Trie with incremented count and reset state.
time_complexity_explanation: Each input call traverses p prefix chars, searches q matching chars in the subtree, and sorts m completions.
space_complexity_explanation: The Trie stores n sentences each of average length L.
key_insight: Tracking the current Trie node as input progresses avoids re-traversing the prefix each time, making incremental search efficient.

title: Verifying an Alien Dictionary
hints: Build a mapping from each alien character to its order index.
For each adjacent pair of words, compare them character by character using the alien order.
If words[i] is a prefix of words[i+1], that is fine; but if words[i+1] is a prefix of words[i], return False.
approach: Create a hash map that maps each character to its position in the alien order string. Then for each consecutive pair of words, compare them character by character. If a character in words[i] comes after words[i+1] in alien order, return False. If words[i] is longer than words[i+1] and is a prefix of it, return False. Otherwise return True.
time_complexity_explanation: We scan each character across all words at most once, where C is the total number of characters.
space_complexity_explanation: The rank map stores at most 26 characters, so it is O(1) space.
key_insight: Mapping each alien character to a numeric rank transforms the problem into a standard lexicographic comparison using the new ordering.

title: Minimum Operations to Reduce X to Zero
hints: Instead of finding the minimum elements to remove from ends, find the maximum subarray whose sum equals total_sum - x.
Use a sliding window to find the longest subarray with sum equal to total_sum - x.
The answer is n minus the length of that subarray; if no such subarray exists, return -1.
approach: Reformulate the problem: removing elements from both ends summing to x is equivalent to finding the longest subarray in the middle with sum = total_sum - x. Use a sliding window with two pointers to find the maximum length subarray with the target sum. The minimum operations is n minus this maximum length.
time_complexity_explanation: The sliding window processes each element at most twice (once added, once removed).
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of pointer and sum variables are used.
key_insight: The complement trick converts a hard two-ended removal problem into a standard maximum-length subarray problem solvable with a sliding window.

title: Maximum Good Subarray Sum
hints: A good subarray has |nums[i] - nums[j]| <= 1 for its first and last elements.
Use prefix sums: the sum of subarray [i..j] = prefix[j+1] - prefix[i].
For each j, look up nums[j]-1, nums[j], and nums[j]+1 in a hash map storing the minimum prefix sum seen for each value.
approach: Compute prefix sums. For each index j (as the end of the subarray), we want to maximize prefix[j+1] - prefix[i] where |nums[i] - nums[j]| <= 1. Maintain a hash map from value to the minimum prefix sum at any index with that value. For each j, check the map for nums[j]-1, nums[j], and nums[j]+1 to find the best starting index.
time_complexity_explanation: We iterate once over the array, doing O(1) hash map lookups per element.
space_complexity_explanation: The prefix array and hash map each store O(n) entries in the worst case.
key_insight: Storing the minimum prefix sum per distinct value allows O(1) lookup for the best starting point satisfying the |diff| <= 1 condition.

title: Flip Square Submatrix Vertically
hints: For each square submatrix, flipping vertically means reversing each row within the submatrix.
Iterate over all possible top-left corners and all valid square sizes.
For each cell after the flip, determine its new value and sum up all 1s.
approach: For each possible square submatrix defined by its top-left corner (r, c) and size k, simulate a vertical flip by reversing each row of the submatrix. Count the number of 1s in the resulting matrix. However, since we want maximum 1s across all possible single flips, try each valid (r, c, k) combination and track the best outcome.
time_complexity_explanation: Iterating all square submatrices takes O(m * n * min(m,n)) time.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant amount of extra space is needed beyond the input.
key_insight: Flipping a submatrix vertically (reversing rows) only swaps values at mirror-image column positions within each row, so the count of 1s in the submatrix does not change.

title: Construct Uniform Parity Array I
hints: The goal is to make all elements have the same parity (all odd or all even) by incrementing by 1 at most once per element.
Count how many elements are already odd and how many are even.
To make all odd: change each even number (costs 1 op each). To make all even: change each odd number (costs 1 op each). Return the minimum.
approach: Count the number of odd and even elements in the array. To make all elements odd, we need to increment each even element by 1 (total operations = count of even numbers). To make all elements even, we need to increment each odd element by 1 (total operations = count of odd numbers). Return the minimum of the two options.
time_complexity_explanation: A single pass over the array counts odd and even elements.
space_complexity_explanation: Only two counter variables are used regardless of input size.
key_insight: Incrementing an even number makes it odd and vice versa, so we can freely convert any element with one operation, making the problem equivalent to choosing the majority parity.

title: Reformat Department Table
hints: Use conditional aggregation (CASE WHEN or IF) to pivot month columns.
Group by id and aggregate the revenue for each month into its own column.
Use MAX() or SUM() around the CASE expression to collapse multiple rows per id into one.
approach: Use a pivot query with conditional aggregation. For each month (Jan through Dec), use a CASE WHEN expression inside MAX() or SUM() to extract revenue only for that month. Group by id so each department gets one output row with all 12 monthly revenue columns.
solution_python:
SELECT id,
    MAX(CASE WHEN month = 'Jan' THEN revenue END) AS Jan_Revenue,
    MAX(CASE WHEN month = 'Feb' THEN revenue END) AS Feb_Revenue,
    MAX(CASE WHEN month = 'Mar' THEN revenue END) AS Mar_Revenue,
    MAX(CASE WHEN month = 'Apr' THEN revenue END) AS Apr_Revenue,
    MAX(CASE WHEN month = 'May' THEN revenue END) AS May_Revenue,
    MAX(CASE WHEN month = 'Jun' THEN revenue END) AS Jun_Revenue,
    MAX(CASE WHEN month = 'Jul' THEN revenue END) AS Jul_Revenue,
    MAX(CASE WHEN month = 'Aug' THEN revenue END) AS Aug_Revenue,
    MAX(CASE WHEN month = 'Sep' THEN revenue END) AS Sep_Revenue,
    MAX(CASE WHEN month = 'Oct' THEN revenue END) AS Oct_Revenue,
    MAX(CASE WHEN month = 'Nov' THEN revenue END) AS Nov_Revenue,
    MAX(CASE WHEN month = 'Dec' THEN revenue END) AS Dec_Revenue
FROM Department
GROUP BY id;
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: The query scans all n rows once to build the pivot aggregation.
space_complexity_explanation: The output has at most as many rows as distinct department ids, proportional to input size.
key_insight: Conditional aggregation with GROUP BY is the standard SQL pivot technique, using MAX() to select the one non-null value per group-month combination.

title: Equal Sum Grid Partition I
hints: Compute the total sum of the grid. A valid partition requires the sum of one half equals half the total sum.
Try every possible horizontal cut (between rows) and vertical cut (between columns). For each, compute the prefix sum of one side.
A horizontal cut after row i is valid if sum of rows 0..i equals totalSum / 2. Same logic for vertical cuts.
approach: Compute the total sum of the entire grid. Then compute prefix row sums and prefix column sums. For each possible horizontal partition (after row i) and vertical partition (after column j), check if the prefix sum of one part equals half the total. If total sum is odd, no valid partition exists.
time_complexity_explanation: We compute the total sum and then scan rows and columns once each, all in O(m*n) time.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of running sum variables are used beyond the input grid.
key_insight: A valid single straight-line partition exists if and only if some prefix of rows (or columns) sums to exactly half the total, which is checkable in a single linear pass.

title: Equal Sum Grid Partition II
hints: Unlike Part I, you may move one element to the boundary between the two partitions — think about what that means for the sum difference.
For a horizontal cut after row i, compute leftSum and rightSum. If they differ by 2*v for some grid value v that lies on the boundary row, we can 'move' that element.
The boundary element for a horizontal cut is in rows i or i+1; for a vertical cut it is in columns j or j+1. Check if the required balancing value exists on the appropriate boundary.
approach: Compute the total grid sum. For each potential cut, compute prefix sums. If leftSum == rightSum the partition is immediately valid. Otherwise, if the difference is even (say diff = leftSum - rightSum), check whether there exists a cell on the partition boundary row (or column) with value diff/2 — moving that cell to the other side balances the partition.
time_complexity_explanation: Each row and column prefix sum is computed in O(m*n) total; boundary set construction is also O(m) or O(n) per cut.
space_complexity_explanation: Sets storing boundary row or column values are at most O(max(m,n)) in size.
key_insight: Allowing one boundary-cell reassignment means a cut is valid when the sum difference equals exactly twice some value present on the partition boundary line.

title: Matrix Similarity After Cyclic Shifts
hints: Simulate the cyclic shifts: odd-indexed rows shift right, even-indexed rows shift left.
After k shifts, an odd row shifted right k times returns to original after n shifts (n = number of columns).
Compare each cell of the shifted matrix to the original. Use modular arithmetic to find where each element ends up.
approach: For each row, determine the effective shift amount using modulo by the row length. Even-indexed rows shift left by k (equivalent to shifting right by n-k), and odd-indexed rows shift right by k. After computing the net shift mod n, check if each row is identical to the original row under that cyclic shift.
time_complexity_explanation: Each cell is visited exactly once to compare against its shifted position.
space_complexity_explanation: No extra data structures are needed beyond a few index variables.
key_insight: Rather than physically rotating the array, use modular index arithmetic to check in-place whether the matrix is self-similar under the cyclic shift.

title: Check if Strings Can be Made Equal With Operations I
hints: The operation swaps characters at positions i and i+2. Notice that only characters at even indices can be swapped with other even-index characters, and odd indices with other odd indices.
Since the strings are short (length 4), just sort the even-indexed characters and odd-indexed characters of each string separately.
After sorting within even and odd index groups, the two strings are equalizable if and only if their sorted groups match.
approach: For each string of length 4, separate the characters at even indices (0, 2) and odd indices (1, 3). Sort each group independently. Two strings can be made equal if and only if the sorted even-index group and sorted odd-index group of s1 match those of s2.
time_complexity_explanation: String length is fixed at 4, so all operations (sorting groups of size 2) are constant time.
space_complexity_explanation: Only fixed-size arrays of length 2 are created for sorting.
key_insight: A swap of positions i and i+2 preserves the parity of indices, so the even-indexed characters and odd-indexed characters form independent groups that can only be rearranged among themselves.

title: Sum of Distances in Tree
hints: First compute the answer for the root using one DFS, tracking subtree sizes and cumulative distances.
Then rerooting: when you move the root from a parent to a child, the distance decreases by subtree_size[child] and increases by (n - subtree_size[child]).
Two DFS passes — one bottom-up to compute subtree sizes and root distances, one top-down to propagate answers to all nodes — gives the full result in O(n).
approach: Use two DFS passes. In the first pass (post-order), compute the size of each subtree and the sum of distances from the root to all nodes. In the second pass (pre-order), use the rerooting technique: for each node v and its parent u, ans[v] = ans[u] - count[v] + (n - count[v]), where count[v] is the subtree size. This propagates the answer from root to all nodes in O(n).
time_complexity_explanation: Two DFS traversals each visit every node and edge exactly once.
space_complexity_explanation: The adjacency list, count array, and ans array each require O(n) space.
key_insight: The rerooting trick allows propagating the root's answer to all other nodes in O(n) by leveraging the relationship between a parent's answer and its child's answer via subtree sizes.

title: Find in Mountain Array
hints: First find the peak index of the mountain array using binary search.
Then binary search in the ascending half (left of peak) for the target.
If not found in the ascending half, binary search in the descending half (right of peak). Return the smaller index if found in the ascending half.
approach: Perform three binary searches. First, find the peak (maximum) index using binary search by comparing adjacent elements. Second, binary search the ascending left portion for the target. Third, if not found, binary search the descending right portion. The problem requires minimizing API calls to the MountainArray interface.
time_complexity_explanation: Three separate binary searches each take O(log n) time, for a total of O(log n).
space_complexity_explanation: Only pointer variables are used; no extra data structures are allocated.
key_insight: A mountain array has exactly one peak that splits it into ascending and descending halves, each searchable by binary search — but the descending half has reversed comparison direction.

title: Check if Strings Can be Made Equal With Operations II
hints: The operation swaps characters at positions i and i+2, which means characters at even positions can only swap with other even-position characters.
Collect all characters at even indices from both strings and sort them; do the same for odd indices.
The two strings are equalizable if and only if their sorted even-index characters match and sorted odd-index characters match.
approach: For strings of arbitrary equal length, characters at even indices can only reach other even-index positions via repeated swaps of distance 2, and similarly for odd indices. Collect characters at even positions and odd positions from each string, sort each group, and check that s1 and s2 have the same multiset of characters at even positions and the same multiset at odd positions.
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting the even and odd character groups each takes O(n/2 * log(n/2)) which simplifies to O(n log n).
space_complexity_explanation: The sorted character arrays require O(n) space in total.
key_insight: Index-parity is an invariant of the swap operation — swapping positions i and i+2 keeps both characters within their parity class — so equalizing the strings reduces to matching multisets within each parity class.

title: Delete the Middle Node of a Linked List
hints: Use the fast and slow pointer technique to find the middle node.
To delete a node, you need a reference to the node before the middle. Consider starting the slow pointer one step behind.
When the fast pointer reaches the end, the slow pointer will be just before the middle node — update slow.next to skip the middle.
approach: Use two pointers: a slow pointer that moves one step at a time and a fast pointer that moves two steps. To delete the middle node, keep a 'prev' pointer one step behind slow. When fast reaches the end of the list, prev.next points to the middle node — skip it by setting prev.next = prev.next.next.
time_complexity_explanation: The fast pointer traverses the list once, visiting each node at most once.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of pointer variables are used regardless of list length.
key_insight: By starting the slow pointer at a dummy node before head, when fast reaches the end, slow is positioned exactly at the node preceding the middle, allowing O(1) deletion.

title: Lexicographically Smallest Generated String
hints: Think greedily: try to place 'a' at each position from left to right whenever it does not violate constraints imposed by str1 and str2.
str1 determines which positions in the result have a fixed substring starting there. If str1[i] = 'T', the substring result[i..i+len2-1] must equal str2.
When placing characters, propagate forward the forced characters from str2 at all 'T' positions, and verify consistency — if a position is forced to two different characters, there is no solution.
approach: Build the result string greedily from left to right. For each position i, if str1[i] == 'T', the window result[i..i+m-1] must be str2 — mark all these positions as forced. If str1[i] == 'F', ensure no forced constraint already set a value; if unconstrained, set it to 'a'. After propagating all constraints, check for conflicts. Use a forward-pass to lock in forced characters and a backward-pass or single pass with a 'last forced T' tracking to ensure the last occurrence of 'T' constraints are satisfied.
time_complexity_explanation: For each of the n positions in str1, we may inspect up to m positions of str2, giving O(n*m) in the worst case.
space_complexity_explanation: The result and forced arrays are each of length n+m-1.
key_insight: Greedily assign 'a' everywhere, then use the 'T' constraints to forcibly overwrite windows with str2, checking conflicts; for 'F' constraints, if the window accidentally matches str2, disrupt it at the rightmost non-forced position to keep lexicographic order minimal.

title: Minimum Add to Make Parentheses Valid
hints: Track two counters: unmatched open parentheses and unmatched close parentheses.
Scan left to right: a ')' with no open to match increments the close counter; an '(' increments the open counter.
The answer is the sum of unmatched opens and unmatched closes at the end.
approach: Use a greedy approach with two counters. Traverse the string maintaining 'open' (unmatched '(' so far) and 'close' (unmatched ')' seen before any '(' to match). For each '(' increment open; for each ')' if open > 0 decrement open (matched), else increment close. The answer is open + close.
time_complexity_explanation: A single left-to-right pass over the string of length n.
space_complexity_explanation: Only two integer counters are maintained regardless of input size.
key_insight: Unmatched closing brackets can never be fixed by later characters, so counting them separately from unmatched opening brackets gives the exact minimum additions needed.

title: Count Odd Numbers in an Interval Range
hints: Think about how many odd numbers exist from 0 to n.
Use a helper: count(n) = (n + 1) // 2 gives odd numbers in [0, n].
The answer is count(high) - count(low - 1).
approach: Define a helper function count(n) that returns the number of odd integers in [0, n], which is simply (n + 1) // 2. The number of odd integers in [low, high] is then count(high) - count(low - 1). This avoids iterating through the range entirely.
time_complexity_explanation: Only arithmetic operations are performed regardless of the range size.
space_complexity_explanation: No extra data structures are used.
key_insight: The count of odd numbers in [0, n] is (n+1)//2, allowing any range query to be answered with a simple subtraction in O(1).

title: Count Submatrices with Top-Left Element and Sum Less Than k
hints: Build a 2D prefix sum table to query any submatrix sum in O(1).
Every valid submatrix must include the top-left cell (0,0), so enumerate all bottom-right corners (i,j).
Use prefix[i][j] = grid[i][j] + prefix[i-1][j] + prefix[i][j-1] - prefix[i-1][j-1].
approach: Compute a 2D prefix sum in-place or in a separate array so that prefix[i][j] holds the sum of the submatrix from (0,0) to (i,j). Then iterate over every cell (i,j); the prefix sum at that cell is the sum of the submatrix with top-left corner at (0,0) and bottom-right at (i,j). Count all cells where this prefix sum is less than k.
time_complexity_explanation: We visit each cell of the m×n grid exactly once to build the prefix sum and count valid submatrices.
space_complexity_explanation: A 2D prefix sum array of size m×n is maintained.
key_insight: Because every valid submatrix must include (0,0), any submatrix is fully described by its bottom-right corner, making a single 2D prefix sum pass sufficient to evaluate all candidates.

title: Maximum Width Ramp
hints: Build a decreasing stack of indices from left to right — these are the only candidates for the left end of a ramp.
Scan from the right end; for each index j, pop from the stack while nums[stack.top()] <= nums[j] and record j - stack.top().
Once a stack index is popped for a larger j, it need not be revisited.
approach: First, build a monotonically decreasing stack of indices by scanning left to right and only pushing an index if its value is strictly less than all previously pushed values. Then scan right to left: for each j, while the stack's top index i satisfies nums[i] <= nums[j], pop it and update the answer with j - i. This two-pass approach finds the maximum width ramp in O(n).
time_complexity_explanation: Each index is pushed and popped from the stack at most once across both passes.
space_complexity_explanation: The monotonic stack can hold at most n indices in the worst case.
key_insight: Candidates for the ramp's left end form a decreasing prefix — any index dominated by a smaller value to its left can never extend the width. Scanning from the right greedily pops and records the farthest valid pairs.

title: Walking Robot Simulation
hints: Store obstacles in a hash set for O(1) lookup.
Maintain the robot's direction as an index into a directions array [North, East, South, West].
Move one step at a time and check for obstacles before updating position.
approach: Encode the four compass directions as unit vectors [N, E, S, W] and use an index into this array to track facing direction (turn right increments index mod 4, turn left decrements mod 4). Store all obstacle coordinates in a hash set as tuples. For each move command, step one unit at a time in the current direction, stopping if the next cell is an obstacle. Track the maximum squared Euclidean distance after each step.
time_complexity_explanation: C is the total number of steps across all move commands and O is the number of obstacles loaded into the hash set.
space_complexity_explanation: The obstacle hash set stores O entries.
key_insight: Storing obstacles in a hash set and moving one step at a time keeps each obstacle check O(1), turning an otherwise brute-force simulation into an efficient linear-time solution.

title: Longest Harmonious Subsequence
hints: Count the frequency of each number with a hash map.
A harmonious subsequence consists of exactly two distinct values that differ by 1.
For each key k in the map, if k+1 also exists, the harmonious subsequence length is freq[k] + freq[k+1].
approach: Build a frequency map counting occurrences of each integer. Then iterate over every key k in the map; if k+1 also exists in the map, the harmonious subsequence formed by k and k+1 has length freq[k] + freq[k+1]. Return the maximum such value found, or 0 if none.
time_complexity_explanation: One pass to build the frequency map and one pass over its keys, both linear in n.
space_complexity_explanation: The frequency map stores at most n distinct keys.
key_insight: Because a harmonious subsequence requires exactly a difference of 1, you only need to check adjacent integer pairs in the frequency map — no sorting or sliding window required.

title: Time Needed to Inform All Employees
hints: Build an adjacency list from manager to list of direct reports.
Run DFS from the head of the company (headID).
The time to inform the subtree rooted at employee i is informTime[i] + max(time for each subordinate).
approach: Build a tree represented as an adjacency list where manager[i] -> list of employees reporting to i. Perform a DFS starting from headID; at each node the total time is informTime[node] plus the maximum DFS result over all its direct reports. The base case (leaf employee) returns 0.
time_complexity_explanation: Each of the n employees is visited exactly once during the DFS.
space_complexity_explanation: The adjacency list and recursion stack together use O(n) space.
key_insight: The total time to inform an employee's entire subtree equals that manager's inform time plus the worst-case time among all branches — a classic tree max-path DFS.

title: Timeout Cancellation
hints: Use setTimeout to schedule the function call.
Return a cancellation function that calls clearTimeout.
If cancelTimeMs elapses before t ms, clearTimeout prevents fn from running.
approach: Schedule fn(...args) with setTimeout using delay t. Return a closure that, when called, invokes clearTimeout on the stored timer ID. If the returned cancel function is called before the timer fires, the function is never executed.
solution_python:
# JavaScript-only problem: Python equivalent shown
import threading

def cancellable(fn, args, t):
    timer = threading.Timer(t / 1000, lambda: fn(*args))
    timer.start()
    def cancel():
        timer.cancel()
    return cancel
time_complexity_explanation: Scheduling a timeout and returning a cancel closure are both constant-time operations.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a single timer reference is stored.
key_insight: JavaScript's event loop model lets setTimeout + clearTimeout act as a natural cancellation mechanism — the cancel function is simply a closure over the timer ID.

title: Escape The Ghosts
hints: The Manhattan distance is the minimum number of moves to reach any target on a grid.
Compute your own Manhattan distance to the target.
If any ghost's Manhattan distance to the target is <= yours, it can intercept you.
approach: The player can escape if and only if their Manhattan distance to the target is strictly less than every ghost's Manhattan distance to the target. This is because each entity moves optimally, and a ghost can always intercept by waiting at the target if it arrives no later than the player. Compare Manhattan distances directly.
time_complexity_explanation: A single pass over the g ghost positions computes each Manhattan distance in O(1).
space_complexity_explanation: Only scalar variables are used regardless of input size.
key_insight: On a grid with no walls, optimal movement is fully captured by Manhattan distance — if a ghost reaches the target no later than you, it can always intercept, so the problem reduces to a single distance comparison per ghost.

title: Odd Even Jump
hints: For each index, precompute the next index reachable on an odd jump (next greater or equal value) and on an even jump (next smaller or equal value).
Use a sorted structure (e.g. sorted keys + binary search, or a TreeMap) to find the target index efficiently.
Define dp_odd[i] and dp_even[i]: can you reach the end starting from i on an odd/even jump? Work right to left.
approach: Precompute two arrays: next_odd[i] = the index you jump to from i on an odd jump (smallest value >= arr[i] among i+1..n-1, leftmost if tie), and next_even[i] = the index you jump to on an even jump (largest value <= arr[i], leftmost if tie). Build these using a monotonic stack on the sorted order of indices. Then use DP from right to left: odd[i] = even[next_odd[i]], even[i] = odd[next_even[i]], with the last index always True. Count indices where odd[i] is True.
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting indices takes O(n log n); the monotonic stack and DP passes are each O(n).
space_complexity_explanation: Four arrays of size n are maintained for next pointers and DP states.
key_insight: The key insight is reducing 'find next valid jump target' to a sorted-order monotonic stack problem: by sorting indices by value, finding the next reachable index becomes a matter of finding the next larger index in that sorted order, solvable with a single stack pass.

title: Minimum Distance Between Three Equal Elements II
hints: Group the indices of each value using a hash map.
For any value with at least 3 occurrences, pick three indices that minimize the span.
The optimal triple of indices is always three consecutive occurrences in the sorted index list.
approach: Build a map from each value to the sorted list of its indices. For each value whose index list has at least 3 entries, slide a window of size 3 over consecutive index triples and compute the cost as indices[i+2] - indices[i] (the span). Return the minimum span found across all values and all windows; return -1 if no value appears at least three times.
time_complexity_explanation: Building the index map and sliding over all triples together visit each element a constant number of times.
space_complexity_explanation: The index map stores all n positions in the worst case.
key_insight: The minimum-cost triple of equal elements is always three consecutive appearances in index order — any non-consecutive triple has a strictly larger or equal span than some consecutive sub-triple.

title: Minimum Operations to Transform Array into Alternating Prime
hints: An alternating array has all even-indexed elements equal and all odd-indexed elements equal, with both values being prime.
Precompute primes up to the maximum value using a sieve, then find the most frequent element at even and odd positions.
For each position, the cost is 1 if the current value differs from the chosen prime, 0 otherwise; minimize total cost by picking the best prime pair.
approach: Use the Sieve of Eratosthenes to precompute all primes. For even-indexed positions and odd-indexed positions separately, count the frequency of each prime. The minimum operations needed is n minus the count of elements already equal to the most frequent prime at even positions plus the most frequent prime at odd positions. The two chosen primes must be different.
time_complexity_explanation: Sieve runs in O(M log log M) where M is the max value; counting elements is O(N).
space_complexity_explanation: The sieve array uses O(M) space proportional to the maximum element value.
key_insight: Only check the top-2 most frequent primes at even and odd positions — the optimal pair must come from these candidates since any other choice can only be worse.

title: Maximize Cyclic Partition Score
hints: Think about partitioning a circular array into contiguous segments and scoring each segment.
For a cyclic array, fix the starting cut point and use DP to find the maximum score partition.
Prefix sums and careful state tracking help handle the wrap-around efficiently.
approach: Model the problem as partitioning a circular array into k non-empty contiguous segments to maximize total score. Duplicate the array to handle circularity, then use dynamic programming where dp[i][j] represents the maximum score using j cuts among the first i elements. Prefix sums allow O(1) segment score queries, making the DP efficient.
time_complexity_explanation: The DP iterates k layers each scanning 2n positions, giving O(nk) total.
space_complexity_explanation: Rolling DP arrays use O(n) space after optimization.
key_insight: By reformulating the DP recurrence to maximize prev[l] - prefix[l] over l, we convert an O(n^2 k) DP into O(nk) using a running maximum.

title: Minimum Deletions to Make Array Divisible
hints: The element of nums that divides all elements of numsDivide must be a divisor of the GCD of numsDivide.
Sort nums and iterate from the smallest element; check if it divides the GCD of numsDivide.
Count how many elements from the front of sorted nums you must delete before finding a valid divisor.
approach: Compute the GCD of all elements in numsDivide. Sort nums. Iterate through sorted nums and for each element check if it divides the GCD. The first such element requires deleting all elements before it. If no element divides the GCD, return -1.
time_complexity: O(n log n + m log M)
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting nums is O(n log n); computing GCD across m elements is O(m log M) where M is the max value.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant amount of extra space is used beyond input.
key_insight: Any valid element in nums must divide the GCD of numsDivide — so you only need to find the smallest such element after sorting, eliminating all smaller ones.

title: Traffic Signal Color
hints: A traffic light cycles through green, yellow, red with given durations.
Use the modulo operation to find which phase a given time falls into.
Sum the durations to find the cycle length, then determine the color from the remainder.
approach: Compute the total cycle length as the sum of green, yellow, and red durations. Use modulo to find the position within the current cycle. Compare against cumulative durations to determine which color is active at the given time.
time_complexity_explanation: All operations are arithmetic with no iteration, so runtime is constant.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of variables are used.
key_insight: Reducing the query time modulo the full cycle length maps any query time to a position within a single cycle, making color determination a simple range check.

title: Minimum Distance to Type a Word Using Two Fingers
hints: Define state as (finger1_position, finger2_position, current_character_index).
At each step, either finger can move to type the next character; compute the cost and recurse.
Use memoization; note that one finger position can be derived implicitly to reduce state space.
approach: Use DP where the state is (index in word, position of finger 1, position of finger 2). At each step, one finger types the current character and moves there. The distance between two keys is computed from their row/column positions on the phone keypad layout. Memoize the recursive calls. The space can be reduced by noting that the previous character is always on one of the two fingers.
time_complexity_explanation: The DP has n * 27 states (index times possible second-finger positions including not placed), each computed in O(1).
space_complexity_explanation: The memoization table stores n * 27 entries.
key_insight: By always tracking which finger just typed the previous character implicitly (it is always on word[i-1]), the state space collapses to just (index, other_finger_position), making the DP tractable.

title: Inverse Coin Change
hints: Given the number of ways to make each amount, reconstruct the coin denominations.
Think about how adding a new coin denomination changes the ways array incrementally.
A coin of value c causes ways[c] to increase by 1 compared to having no coin of that value.
approach: Iterate through the ways array and identify positions where the count increased compared to what a standard coin-change DP would produce without that coin. Simulate the coin-change DP incrementally: try adding a coin of each denomination and check if it produces the observed ways array. The coin is valid if the resulting DP matches.
time_complexity_explanation: For each potential coin denomination up to n, updating the DP array takes O(n) time.
space_complexity_explanation: The DP array uses O(n) space.
key_insight: The standard coin-change DP is reversible: by scanning denominations in order, a discrepancy between the expected and observed count at position c signals a coin of value c, which is then incorporated into the DP.

title: Closest Equal Element Queries
hints: Group indices by their values using a hash map.
For a query index, find the value at that index, look up its index list, and binary search for the nearest occurrence.
Account for the circular nature of the array when computing distances.
approach: Precompute a map from each value to the sorted list of its indices. For each query index i, find value nums[i], look up its index list, and binary search for position i. The closest equal element is either the immediate predecessor or successor in the index list, considering circular wrap-around by comparing distances.
solution_python:
from collections import defaultdict
import bisect

class Solution:
    def solveQueries(self, nums: list[int], queries: list[int]) -> list[int]:
        n = len(nums)
        idx_map = defaultdict(list)
        for i, v in enumerate(nums):
            idx_map[v].append(i)
        
        ans = []
        for q in queries:
            v = nums[q]
            idxs = idx_map[v]
            if len(idxs) == 1:
                ans.append(-1)
                continue
            pos = bisect.bisect_left(idxs, q)
            best = float('inf')
            for delta in [-1, 0, 1]:
                p = (pos + delta) % len(idxs)
                j = idxs[p]
                if j != q:
                    d = min(abs(j - q), n - abs(j - q))
                    best = min(best, d)
            ans.append(best if best < float('inf') else -1)
        return ans
time_complexity_explanation: Building the index map is O(n); each of q queries does a binary search in O(log n).
space_complexity_explanation: The index map stores all n indices distributed across value groups.
key_insight: Grouping indices by value and binary searching within each group gives O(log n) per query; checking only the two neighbors in the sorted index list with circular wrap is sufficient to find the closest.

title: Mirror Distance of an Integer
hints: The mirror of an integer is formed by reversing its digits.
Compute the absolute difference between the number and its digit-reversal.
Be careful with leading zeros when reversing — the mirror may have fewer digits.
approach: Convert the integer to a string, reverse the string to get the mirror, convert back to integer (which handles leading zeros), then return the absolute difference between the original and mirror integer.
time_complexity_explanation: Reversing d digits (where d is the number of digits) takes O(d) time.
space_complexity_explanation: String representation uses O(d) space proportional to number of digits.
key_insight: Converting to string for reversal elegantly handles leading zeros in the mirror — int('0123') yields 123, correctly representing the mirrored value.

title: Final Value of Variable After Performing Operations
hints: Parse each operation string to determine if it increments or decrements X.
Operations containing '++' increment X by 1; operations containing '--' decrement X by 1.
Simply iterate through all operations and accumulate the result.
approach: Initialize a variable X to 0. Iterate through each operation string. If the operation contains '++', increment X; if it contains '--', decrement X. Return the final value of X.
time_complexity_explanation: A single pass through the n operations performs a constant-time check per operation.
space_complexity_explanation: Only one integer variable is maintained regardless of input size.
key_insight: All four operation forms ('++X', 'X++', '--X', 'X--') can be distinguished by simply checking for the presence of '+' or '-', making parsing trivial.

title: Minimize Hamming Distance After Swap Operations
hints: Model allowed swaps as edges in a graph; indices in the same connected component can be freely rearranged.
Use Union-Find to group indices that can reach each other through the allowed swaps.
For each connected component, greedily match source values to target values to minimize mismatches.
approach: Use Union-Find to group source indices by connectivity through allowed swaps. For each connected component, collect the multiset of source[i] values and target[i] values. Greedily cancel matching values using a Counter: for each value present in source, subtract from the target Counter. Elements that cannot be matched contribute to the Hamming distance.
time_complexity_explanation: Union-Find operations with path compression are near O(1) each; processing all components visits each index once.
space_complexity_explanation: The Union-Find structure and group maps use O(n) space.
key_insight: Within each connected component, elements can be freely rearranged — so the minimum Hamming distance contribution from a component is the size of the component minus the size of the intersection of its source and target multisets.

title: Words Within Two Edits of Dictionary
hints: For each query word, compare it character by character with every dictionary word.
Count the number of positions where the two words differ — if it is 2 or fewer the query is valid.
Because all words have the same length you can short-circuit as soon as the difference count exceeds 2.
approach: Since all words share the same length, compare each query word against every dictionary word by counting character mismatches. If any dictionary word differs in at most 2 positions, the query word is included in the result. Short-circuit comparison once mismatches exceed 2 to improve practical performance.
time_complexity_explanation: For each of q query words we compare against each of d dictionary words character by character up to length L.
space_complexity_explanation: The output list holds at most q query words.
key_insight: Because all words have the same length, edit distance reduces to simple Hamming distance (substitutions only), making the comparison a straightforward character-by-character scan.

title: Two Furthest Houses With Different Colors
hints: The maximum distance pair must include either the first or the last house.
Scan from the right looking for any house with a different color than colors[0], then scan from the left looking for any house with a different color than colors[n-1].
Take the maximum of the two distances found.
approach: The optimal pair always involves the first or the last house. Scan from right to left to find the rightmost house whose color differs from colors[0], recording that distance. Scan from left to right to find the leftmost house whose color differs from colors[n-1], recording that distance. Return the maximum of the two.
time_complexity_explanation: Two linear scans over the array of n houses are performed.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of variables are used regardless of input size.
key_insight: The furthest pair must involve one endpoint of the array; checking both endpoints and greedily scanning inward from each gives the answer in two passes.

title: Sum of Distances
hints: Group indices by their value; all same-value indices form a sorted list.
For a group of size k, compute prefix sums so each element's contribution can be found in O(1).
Use the formula: left sum contribution = index * (position in group) - prefix[position], right = (suffix[position]) - index * (k - position - 1).
approach: Group indices by their value. For each group, maintain a running prefix sum of indices. For the i-th element in the group (0-indexed), the sum of absolute distances to all other elements in the group equals index * i - prefixSum (left part) plus (suffixSum - index * (count - i - 1)) (right part). Accumulate these into the answer array.
solution_python:
from collections import defaultdict

class Solution:
    def distance(self, nums: list[int]) -> list[int]:
        n = len(nums)
        ans = [0] * n
        groups = defaultdict(list)
        for i, v in enumerate(nums):
            groups[v].append(i)
        for indices in groups.values():
            k = len(indices)
            prefix = 0
            for pos, idx in enumerate(indices):
                ans[idx] += idx * pos - prefix
                prefix += idx
            suffix = 0
            for pos in range(k - 1, -1, -1):
                idx = indices[pos]
                right_count = k - 1 - pos
                ans[idx] += suffix - idx * right_count
                suffix += idx
        return ans
time_complexity_explanation: Each index is visited a constant number of times across the grouping and two-pass prefix/suffix steps.
space_complexity_explanation: The groups map and answer array together use O(n) space.
key_insight: Instead of computing pairwise distances naively in O(n^2), use prefix sums within each sorted group to compute each element's total distance contribution in O(1) amortized.

title: Robot Return to Origin
hints: Track the robot's x and y coordinates as you process each move.
Count the number of U/D and L/R moves separately.
The robot returns to origin if and only if the counts of U equals D and L equals R.
approach: Simulate the robot's movements by counting the occurrences of each direction. The robot returns to the origin if and only if the number of 'U' moves equals 'D' moves and the number of 'L' moves equals 'R' moves. This can be done in a single pass.
time_complexity_explanation: The string of n moves is scanned a constant number of times.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of counters are maintained.
key_insight: Net displacement in each axis must be zero; counting moves per direction and checking equality is sufficient — no actual coordinate simulation is needed.

title: Furthest Point From Origin
hints: Count the number of 'L', 'R', and '_' (wildcard) moves.
The net displacement ignoring wildcards is |count('L') - count('R')|.
Assign all wildcards to whichever direction (L or R) has more moves; if tied, assign them all to either direction.
approach: Count occurrences of 'L', 'R', and '_'. The furthest reachable position is the absolute difference between the L-count and R-count plus the number of wildcards, because all wildcards can be assigned to the dominant direction. Return this sum.
time_complexity_explanation: A single pass counts each character type in the moves string of length n.
space_complexity_explanation: Only three integer counters are maintained regardless of input length.
key_insight: All wildcard '_' moves should be assigned to the currently dominant direction; the maximum distance is simply the imbalance between L and R plus the total wildcards.

title: Find Indices of Stable Mountains
hints: A mountain at index i is stable if height[i-1] > threshold.
Iterate from index 1 to n-1 and check the previous element.
Collect all qualifying indices into the result list.
approach: Iterate through indices 1 to n-1. For each index i, check whether height[i-1] exceeds the threshold. If so, index i is a stable mountain and is added to the result list. This is a single linear scan.
time_complexity_explanation: A single pass over the n elements checks each predecessor once.
space_complexity_explanation: The output holds at most k stable mountain indices where k <= n.
key_insight: Stability depends solely on the immediately preceding element; there is no need to look further back or maintain any running state.

title: Convert 1D Array Into 2D Array
hints: First verify that original.length == m * n; if not, return an empty array.
Fill the 2D matrix row by row using integer division and modulo to map flat index to (row, col).
Python list slicing can build each row in one expression.
approach: Check that the total number of elements equals m * n; return an empty list if not. Otherwise, construct the 2D matrix by slicing the original array into m consecutive chunks of size n, which directly forms the rows of the result matrix.
time_complexity_explanation: Every element of the original array is placed exactly once into the output matrix.
space_complexity_explanation: The output 2D array contains m * n elements.
key_insight: The flat-to-2D mapping is trivially achieved with slicing or index arithmetic; the only guard needed is checking total element count.

title: Maximize the Distance Between Points on a Square
hints: Binary search on the answer: the minimum distance d between any two consecutive chosen points.
For a fixed d, greedily check if k points can be chosen on the square's perimeter such that consecutive points are at least d apart.
Represent all candidate points by their arc-length position along the perimeter, then greedily pick the next valid point.
approach: Binary search on the minimum gap d between consecutive chosen points. For a given d, map all candidate points to their perimeter arc-length positions and sort them. Use a greedy scan starting from each candidate as the first point, advancing to the next point at least d ahead along the circular perimeter, and check if k points can be selected. If any starting point allows k selections, d is feasible.
time_complexity_explanation: Binary search runs O(log(perimeter/k)) iterations, and each feasibility check does an O(n^2) greedy scan in the worst case.
space_complexity_explanation: The sorted arc-length array uses O(n) space.
key_insight: Parameterize the square's perimeter as a 1D circular arc; then binary search on the minimum gap d and greedily verify feasibility by advancing along the sorted arc positions.

title: Detect Cycles in 2D Grid
hints: Treat each cell as a graph node; add edges between adjacent cells that share the same character.
Use DFS (or BFS) with a visited array; if you reach an already-visited cell that is not the parent you came from, a cycle exists.
Alternatively, use Union-Find: if two same-character adjacent cells are already in the same component, a cycle is detected.
approach: Use DFS on the grid. Mark cells as visited. For each unvisited cell, start a DFS tracking the parent cell to avoid immediately backtracking. If during the DFS we encounter a visited cell that is not the parent (and has the same character), a cycle is detected. Return True as soon as any cycle is found.
time_complexity_explanation: Each cell is visited at most once during the DFS traversal.
space_complexity_explanation: The visited array and DFS call stack each use O(m * n) space in the worst case.
key_insight: The classic cycle-detection trick in undirected graph DFS applies directly: if a neighbor is already visited and is not the immediate parent, a cycle exists.

title: Check if There is a Valid Path in a Grid
hints: Each street type has a fixed set of two directions it connects; encode these as a lookup table.
Use BFS/DFS: from each reachable cell, follow only connections allowed by the current cell's street type and verify the neighbor's street type connects back.
Two cells are validly connected if the current cell's exit direction matches the neighbor cell's entry direction.
approach: Encode each of the 6 street types as a pair of directions it connects. Perform a BFS/DFS starting from (0,0). From each visited cell, try all directions the current street type allows. Move to the neighbor only if that neighbor's street type also connects back toward the current cell. Return whether (m-1, n-1) is ever reached.
time_complexity_explanation: Each cell is enqueued at most once, so the BFS visits O(m * n) cells total.
space_complexity_explanation: The visited array and BFS queue each hold up to O(m * n) entries.
key_insight: Model street connectivity as a directed adjacency lookup; two adjacent cells are connected if and only if each cell's street type allows movement toward the other, making BFS straightforward.

title: Remove All Adjacent Duplicates In String
hints: Think about using a stack to track characters as you iterate through the string.
If the top of the stack matches the current character, pop it (they form a duplicate pair).
Otherwise, push the current character. The stack contents at the end form the result.
approach: Use a stack to process characters one by one. For each character, if the stack is non-empty and the top equals the current character, pop the top (removing the adjacent duplicate pair). Otherwise, push the current character. After processing all characters, join the stack to form the result.
time_complexity_explanation: Each character is pushed and popped at most once, so the total work is linear in the length of the string.
space_complexity_explanation: The stack can hold up to O(n) characters in the worst case when there are no adjacent duplicates.
key_insight: A stack naturally handles adjacent duplicate removal: whenever the incoming character matches the top, they cancel each other out immediately, eliminating the need for repeated passes.

title: Apply Substitutions
hints: Store all substitution rules in a dictionary keyed by the variable name.
When expanding a variable, it may reference other variables, so expand recursively.
Use memoization to avoid re-expanding the same variable multiple times.
approach: Build a dictionary mapping each variable to its template string. To resolve a variable, replace every placeholder in its template with the recursively resolved value of the referenced variable. Use memoization so each variable is fully expanded at most once, avoiding redundant work.
time_complexity_explanation: In the worst case each variable's expanded value has length proportional to the total output size; memoization ensures each unique template string is expanded only once.
space_complexity_explanation: The memoization cache stores the expanded result for each variable, which can total O(n * m) characters across all entries.
key_insight: Memoizing expanded values ensures that deeply nested or repeated variable references do not cause exponential blowup in expansion time.

title: Partition Array for Maximum Sum
hints: Define dp[i] as the maximum sum achievable for the first i elements.
For each position i, try ending a partition of size 1 to k at position i.
The value contributed by a partition ending at i is max(arr[i-j..i]) * j for each valid j.
approach: Use dynamic programming where dp[i] represents the maximum sum of arr[0..i-1] after partitioning. For each index i, iterate over the last j elements (1 <= j <= k) that form the final partition, compute max of those elements times j, and update dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[i-j] + max_val * j). Return dp[n].
time_complexity_explanation: For each of the n positions we consider up to k partition endings, yielding O(n * k) total work.
space_complexity_explanation: The dp array requires O(n) extra space beyond the input.
key_insight: Within any partition of size up to k, all elements are replaced by the maximum, so the contribution is max * length — this makes a simple DP recurrence exact and efficient.

title: Maximum Score From Grid Operations
hints: Think column by column: the decision for each column depends on how many rows are colored in the current vs previous column.
Use prefix sums to quickly compute the sum of any column range.
Define a DP state over (column, number of rows colored in previous column) to avoid recomputation.
approach: Process the grid column by column. Build a prefix sum for each column. Define dp[j][r] as the maximum score when we have finished column j and colored the top r rows of column j. For each column, try all possible row counts for the current column and the previous column, using prefix sums to compute the score contribution (cells in current column not overlapping with previous column's coloring), and transition optimally.
time_complexity_explanation: For each of the m columns we iterate over all O(n^2) pairs of (previous row count, current row count), giving O(m * n^2) total.
space_complexity_explanation: Prefix sums require O(m * n) space; the DP array itself is O(n).
key_insight: The key is recognizing that the score from a column depends only on how many contiguous top rows are colored in adjacent columns, reducing the state space to a single integer per column.

title: Delete Nodes And Return Forest
hints: Use post-order DFS so children are processed before parents.
If a node is to be deleted, its non-deleted children become new roots of the forest.
A node is a root of a new tree if it is not deleted and either is the original root or its parent was deleted.
approach: Perform a post-order DFS. Maintain a set of nodes to delete. After processing children, if the current node should be deleted, add its non-null children to the result list as new roots and return None to detach it from its parent. If the current node is not deleted and has no parent (or its parent was deleted), add it to the result. Return the list of root nodes.
time_complexity_explanation: Each node is visited exactly once during the DFS traversal.
space_complexity_explanation: The recursion stack and the delete set each use O(n) space in the worst case.
key_insight: Post-order DFS cleanly handles deletion: by the time we process a node, its subtrees are already cleaned up, and returning None automatically severs the link from parent to deleted child.

title: Maximum Path Score in a Grid
hints: Movement is only right or down, so you can use standard grid DP.
The score at each cell is the product of the current cell value and the step number.
Keep track of the step count (which is row + col + 1 for a 0-indexed grid starting at (0,0)).
approach: Use dynamic programming on the grid where dp[i][j] holds the maximum score reachable at cell (i, j). The step number when reaching (i, j) is i + j + 1. Transition from the cell above or the cell to the left by taking the max predecessor score and adding grid[i][j] * (i + j + 1). Return dp[m-1][n-1].
time_complexity_explanation: Each cell is computed exactly once in a single pass through the grid.
space_complexity_explanation: The DP table requires O(m * n) space, which can be optimized to O(n) with a rolling array.
key_insight: The step number is entirely determined by position (i + j + 1), so the score contribution of each cell is fixed regardless of the path taken to reach it, enabling a clean DP recurrence.

title: Find Pivot Index
hints: Compute the total sum of the array first.
As you iterate, maintain a running left sum. The right sum is total - left - nums[i].
The pivot index is where left sum equals right sum.
approach: Compute the total sum of the array. Iterate through the array maintaining a running left sum. At each index, compute the right sum as total - left_sum - nums[i]. If left_sum equals right_sum, return the current index. Update left_sum by adding nums[i]. Return -1 if no pivot is found.
time_complexity_explanation: Two linear passes are made: one to compute the total sum and one to find the pivot index.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant amount of extra space is used beyond the input array.
key_insight: Rather than recomputing left and right sums independently for each index (O(n^2)), you can derive the right sum from the precomputed total and the running left sum in O(1) per step.

title: Restaurant Growth
hints: Aggregate daily totals first, then use a window function or self-join to compute 7-day moving sums.
Only include rows where at least 7 days of data are available (day >= 6 in 0-indexed terms).
Divide the 7-day sum by 7 and round to 2 decimal places for the average.
approach: First aggregate total amount per day. Then compute a 7-day rolling sum and average using a window function with ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW. Filter to only return rows where at least 7 days of history exist. Round the average to 2 decimal places and order by visited_on.
solution_python:
SELECT visited_on,
       SUM(amount) OVER w AS amount,
       ROUND(AVG(amount) OVER w, 2) AS average_amount
FROM (
    SELECT visited_on, SUM(amount) AS amount
    FROM Customer
    GROUP BY visited_on
) daily
WHERE visited_on >= (
    SELECT MIN(visited_on) + INTERVAL 6 DAY FROM Customer
)
WINDOW w AS (ORDER BY visited_on ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)
ORDER BY visited_on;
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting by visited_on for the window function dominates and costs O(n log n).
space_complexity_explanation: The intermediate daily aggregation and window computation require O(n) extra space.
key_insight: Aggregating to daily totals first simplifies the window function: each row in the window represents exactly one day, making the 7-row window correspond directly to a 7-day period.

title: Rotated Digits
hints: A number is valid if it is different after rotation and every digit is valid after rotation.
Digits 0, 1, 8 map to themselves; digits 2->5, 5->2, 6->9, 9->6; digits 3, 4, 7 are invalid.
A number is good if it has no invalid digits and at least one digit that maps to a different digit.
approach: For each number from 1 to n, check if it is a good number. A number is good if: (1) none of its digits are in {3, 4, 7}, and (2) at least one digit is in {2, 5, 6, 9} (ensuring the rotation is different). Iterate through all numbers and count those satisfying both conditions.
time_complexity_explanation: We check each of the n numbers, and each check processes O(log n) digits, giving O(n log n) total.
space_complexity_explanation: Converting a number to a string uses O(log n) space for its digits.
key_insight: The problem reduces to classifying digits into three buckets — invalid (3,4,7), identity (0,1,8), and transforming (2,5,6,9) — and a number is good if and only if it has no invalid digits and at least one transforming digit.

title: Search in a Binary Search Tree
hints: Use BST property: if target < node.val go left, if target > node.val go right.
Recurse or iterate until you find the target or reach a null node.
Return the subtree rooted at the found node, not just True/False.
approach: Leverage the BST property at each node: if the target value equals the current node's value, return the node. If the target is less, recurse into the left subtree; if greater, recurse into the right subtree. Return None if the node is null (value not found).
time_complexity_explanation: The search visits at most h nodes along a single root-to-leaf path where h is the height of the BST (O(log n) balanced, O(n) worst case).
space_complexity_explanation: The recursive call stack depth equals the height h of the tree; iterative implementation reduces this to O(1).
key_insight: The BST invariant allows binary elimination of half the remaining tree at each step, making search far more efficient than a linear scan.

title: Number of Closed Islands
hints: An island is 'closed' if it is completely surrounded by water (1s) and does not touch the boundary.
Try flood-filling all land connected to the boundary first to eliminate open islands.
After eliminating boundary-connected land, count remaining islands using DFS/BFS.
approach: First, flood-fill (mark as visited) all land cells (0s) connected to the grid boundary, since those cannot form closed islands. Then iterate over all remaining unvisited cells: each unvisited land cell starts a new DFS/BFS that marks all connected land cells, incrementing the closed island count by 1 per connected component found.
time_complexity_explanation: Each cell is visited at most twice (once in boundary elimination, once in island counting), giving O(m*n) total work.
space_complexity_explanation: The DFS call stack can be at most O(m*n) deep in the worst case of a grid filled with land.
key_insight: The trick is to first 'drown' all land connected to the boundary — only the truly enclosed land components remain, each of which is exactly one closed island.

title: Create a DataFrame from List
hints: Use pd.DataFrame() to construct a DataFrame directly from the list.
Pass column names as a list to the 'columns' parameter.
Each inner list (or row) becomes one row in the resulting DataFrame.
approach: Use the pandas DataFrame constructor to convert the 2D list of student data into a DataFrame. Pass the column names ['student_id', 'age'] explicitly so the resulting table has the correct headers. The constructor handles the conversion directly in one call.
solution_js: // Pandas problem: Python-only solution
time_complexity_explanation: Constructing the DataFrame requires iterating over all n rows of input data once.
space_complexity_explanation: The resulting DataFrame stores all n rows, using O(n) space.
key_insight: The pandas DataFrame constructor directly accepts a 2D list; specifying column names in the constructor is all that is needed to produce the correctly formatted output.

title: Number of Unique Subjects Taught by Each Teacher
hints: Group rows by teacher_id to aggregate per-teacher statistics.
Use COUNT(DISTINCT ...) to avoid counting the same subject twice for a teacher.
The Teacher table already has one row per (teacher_id, subject_id, dept_id) combination.
approach: Group the Teacher table by teacher_id and use COUNT(DISTINCT subject_id) to compute how many unique subjects each teacher teaches. Since the same subject can appear in multiple departments for the same teacher, DISTINCT is essential to avoid double-counting.
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: A single scan of the Teacher table with grouping takes O(n) time where n is the number of rows.
space_complexity_explanation: The output has one row per distinct teacher_id, so O(k) space where k is the number of unique teachers.
key_insight: COUNT(DISTINCT subject_id) within a GROUP BY teacher_id elegantly handles teachers who teach the same subject in multiple departments.

title: Minimum Cost For Tickets
hints: Define dp[i] as the minimum cost to cover all travel days up to day i.
On a non-travel day, dp[i] = dp[i-1] since no new pass is needed.
On a travel day, consider buying a 1-day, 7-day, or 30-day pass and take the minimum.
approach: Use a DP array indexed by day (1 to 365). For non-travel days, carry forward the previous cost. For travel days, compute the minimum of: dp[i-1] + cost[0] (1-day pass), dp[max(0, i-7)] + cost[1] (7-day pass), dp[max(0, i-30)] + cost[2] (30-day pass). The answer is dp[365].
time_complexity_explanation: W = 365 is constant (days in a year), so the DP runs in O(365) = O(1) effectively, but O(W) in the general sense.
space_complexity_explanation: The dp array has W+1 entries, requiring O(W) space.
key_insight: By indexing DP on calendar day rather than on the index into the days array, non-travel days naturally propagate the previous minimum cost, simplifying the recurrence.

title: Rotating the Box
hints: First apply gravity: within each row, make stones fall to the right (toward the end of the row).
Use a two-pointer approach to efficiently slide stones past empty cells and obstacles.
After gravity is applied, rotate the entire matrix 90 degrees clockwise.
approach: Process each row independently: use a pointer tracking the next available empty slot from the right, moving stones rightward past empty spaces while stopping at obstacles. After simulating gravity in all rows, rotate the matrix 90 degrees clockwise by transposing and reversing rows (or equivalently, placing row r, col c at position col, (rows-1-r) in the result).
time_complexity_explanation: Both the gravity simulation and the rotation pass visit every cell exactly once, giving O(m*n) total.
space_complexity_explanation: The rotated result matrix requires O(m*n) additional space.
key_insight: Decoupling the two operations — first apply gravity within each row using a right-side pointer, then rotate — makes the implementation straightforward and avoids complex diagonal logic.

title: Jump Game IX
hints: Think about which indices can reach which other indices directly based on the jump rules.
Use a monotonic stack to efficiently find the range of indices reachable from each position.
Model the reachability as a graph and find the minimum cost path to the last index.
approach: Build a graph of direct jump connections using two monotonic stacks — one for forward same-sign jumps and one for backward opposite-sign jumps — then run a BFS/Dijkstra-style traversal (or Greedy with a priority queue) to find the minimum cost path from index 0 to index n-1. The cost to reach an index is the sum of costs along the path.
time_complexity_explanation: Building the graph with monotonic stacks takes O(n); Dijkstra over at most O(n) edges runs in O(n log n).
space_complexity_explanation: The graph, distance array, and heap each use O(n) space.
key_insight: Using monotonic stacks to precompute jump targets reduces the number of edges in the graph from O(n^2) to O(n), making the shortest-path search tractable.

title: Minimum Falling Path Sum
hints: Process the matrix row by row; for each cell, consider the minimum cost to reach it from the row above.
From row i-1, column j, you can move to row i at columns j-1, j, or j+1.
You can modify the matrix in-place or use a separate DP table.
approach: Use dynamic programming where dp[i][j] represents the minimum sum to reach cell (i, j) from any cell in the first row. For each cell in row i, take the minimum of the three cells diagonally above it from row i-1 and add the current cell value. The answer is the minimum value in the last row.
time_complexity_explanation: We visit every cell in the n x n matrix exactly once, giving O(n^2) time.
space_complexity_explanation: The DP is done in-place on the input matrix, so no additional space is required beyond O(1) variables.
key_insight: In-place modification of the matrix row-by-row avoids allocating an extra DP table while correctly propagating minimum path sums from top to bottom.

title: Number of Recent Calls
hints: Keep a queue of all request timestamps seen so far.
On each ping(t), add t to the queue and remove all timestamps older than t - 3000.
The queue length after cleanup is the answer.
approach: Maintain a deque of request times. On each call to ping(t), append t to the deque. Then pop from the front of the deque while the oldest element is less than t - 3000. The number of remaining elements is the count of requests in the last 3000 milliseconds.
time_complexity_explanation: Each timestamp is added to and removed from the queue at most once, so the total work across all pings is O(n) amortized O(1) per call.
space_complexity_explanation: The queue holds at most W = 3000 milliseconds worth of requests, bounded by the sliding window size.
key_insight: A queue naturally models a sliding window of recent events — append new arrivals at the back and evict expired entries from the front in O(1) per operation.

title: Water Bottles
hints: Simulate the process: drink numBottles full bottles, then exchange empty ones.
Each round you drink numBottles full bottles and get numBottles // numExchange new full bottles.
Continue until you have fewer full bottles than numExchange.
approach: Simulate the process iteratively: drink all full bottles (adding to total), exchange as many empties as possible to get new full bottles, and carry over any remaining empties. Repeat until you can no longer make an exchange. Alternatively, use the closed-form formula: total = numBottles + (numBottles - 1) / (numExchange - 1).
solution_python:
class Solution:
    def numWaterBottles(self, numBottles: int, numExchange: int) -> int:
        total = 0
        empty = 0
        full = numBottles
        while full > 0:
            total += full
            empty += full
            full = empty // numExchange
            empty = empty % numExchange
        return total
time_complexity_explanation: Each exchange round reduces the number of full bottles by a factor of numExchange, so the loop runs O(log_{numExchange} n) times.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of variables are used regardless of input size.
key_insight: Tracking empties separately and performing integer division each round cleanly simulates the exchange process; there is also a one-line closed-form solution for this specific problem.

title: Exclusive Time of Functions
hints: Use a stack to track the currently executing function at each point in time.
When a function starts, push it; when it ends, pop it and accumulate its exclusive time.
Remember to subtract nested function time from the parent's exclusive time.
approach: Parse each log entry into (function_id, type, timestamp). Maintain a stack of active function IDs and a variable tracking the previous timestamp. On a 'start' event, if the stack is non-empty, credit the elapsed time since prev_time to the current top function, then push the new function. On an 'end' event, credit elapsed time (end - prev_time + 1) to the current top function and pop it, setting prev_time to end + 1.
solution_python:
from typing import List

class Solution:
    def exclusiveTime(self, n: int, logs: List[str]) -> List[int]:
        result = [0] * n
        stack = []
        prev_time = 0
        for log in logs:
            fid, typ, t = log.split(':')
            fid, t = int(fid), int(t)
            if typ == 'start':
                if stack:
                    result[stack[-1]] += t - prev_time
                stack.append(fid)
                prev_time = t
            else:  # end
                result[stack.pop()] += t - prev_time + 1
                prev_time = t + 1
        return result
time_complexity_explanation: Each log entry is processed exactly once, where m is the total number of log entries.
space_complexity_explanation: The stack holds at most n function IDs in the worst case of maximally nested calls.
key_insight: The key is tracking prev_time as the start of the current unaccounted interval — on each event, accumulate the time since prev_time to the currently running function (top of stack) before updating the stack.

title: Minimum Jumps to Reach End via Prime Teleportation
hints: Think of positions as nodes in a graph — you can jump to adjacent positions or teleport to positions sharing a prime factor.
Use a sieve to find all prime factors for each number, then group indices by shared prime factors.
BFS from index 0 gives the shortest path (minimum jumps) to the last index.
approach: Model the problem as a shortest-path (BFS) problem. Each index is a node; edges exist between adjacent indices (cost 1) and between any two indices whose values share a prime factor (cost 1). Use a sieve-like factorization to group indices by prime factors. Run multi-source BFS expanding via adjacency and via the prime-factor groups, marking each prime group as visited once to avoid re-expansion.
time_complexity_explanation: Factorizing each of n numbers takes O(sqrt(max_val)) each; BFS visits each node and prime group at most once.
space_complexity_explanation: Space for the prime-to-index map scales with total prime factor occurrences P across all numbers, plus O(n) for the BFS queue and distance array.
key_insight: Marking an entire prime-factor group as visited after its first BFS expansion prevents redundant re-visits, collapsing what could be O(n^2) teleportation edges into effectively O(n) work.

title: Sum of Beauty of All Substrings
hints: The beauty of a substring is max_freq - min_freq; try all substrings.
Fix the left boundary and extend the right boundary, maintaining a frequency map.
To find min non-zero frequency efficiently, track a frequency-of-frequency map.
approach: Enumerate every substring by fixing the left index i and sweeping the right index j from i to n-1. Maintain a character frequency map; also maintain a frequency-of-frequencies map to retrieve the maximum frequency in O(1). For minimum frequency, iterate over the 26-character frequency map. Sum up max_freq - min_freq for each substring.
time_complexity_explanation: There are O(n^2) substrings; computing max and min over at most 26 character frequencies takes O(26) per substring.
space_complexity_explanation: The frequency map stores at most 26 lowercase English letters regardless of substring length.
key_insight: Because the alphabet is fixed at 26 characters, scanning all character frequencies for min/max is O(1) in practice, making the O(n^2) double-loop the dominant cost.

title: Cyclically Rotating a Grid
hints: Extract each concentric ring (layer) of the matrix into a 1D array.
Rotate the 1D array by k positions (use modulo to handle large k).
Write the rotated values back into the corresponding ring positions.
approach: The grid consists of min(m,n)/2 concentric rectangular rings.
