For each ring, extract all elements in clockwise order into a list, rotate the list by k % len(ring) positions to the right, then write the rotated values back into the grid in the same clockwise order. This cleanly decouples the rotation from the indexing logic.
time_complexity_explanation: Each cell is visited a constant number of times (extract + write back), giving O(m*n) total work.
space_complexity_explanation: The largest ring has O(m+n) elements, which is the maximum size of the temporary ring array.
key_insight: Flattening each concentric ring into a 1D list reduces the rotation to a simple array slice operation, avoiding complex in-place index arithmetic.

title: Factorial Trailing Zeroes
hints: Trailing zeroes come from factors of 10 = 2 * 5; there are always more factors of 2 than 5 in n!.
Count how many multiples of 5 are in n!, then multiples of 25, 125, etc.
Sum floor(n/5) + floor(n/25) + floor(n/125) + ... until the term is zero.
approach: Trailing zeroes in n! are produced by factors of 10, each requiring one factor of 2 and one factor of 5. Since factors of 2 are always more abundant, the count of trailing zeroes equals the count of factors of 5 in n!. This is computed by summing floor(n/5^k) for k=1,2,3,... until 5^k > n.
time_complexity_explanation: The loop runs log_5(n) times since power multiplies by 5 each iteration.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of variables are used.
key_insight: The number of times 5 divides n! is floor(n/5) + floor(n/25) + ... because every multiple of 25 contributes an extra factor of 5 beyond what floor(n/5) counts, and similarly for higher powers.

title: Day of the Year
hints: Parse the year, month, and day from the date string.
Sum the days in all months before the given month.
Remember to add 1 to February's count if the year is a leap year.
approach: Parse the date string to extract year, month, and day. Maintain a list of days per month. Determine if the year is a leap year (divisible by 4 but not 100, or divisible by 400) and adjust February accordingly. Sum the days of all complete months before the given month, then add the day component.
time_complexity_explanation: The computation involves a fixed-size array of 12 months, so all operations are constant time.
space_complexity_explanation: Only the fixed-length 12-element month array and a few integer variables are used.
key_insight: Leap year detection requires checking divisibility by 400 as an override to the 100-year exception, which is the most common source of bugs in this problem.

title: Separate the Digits in an Array
hints: Convert each integer to its string representation to access individual digits.
Iterate through each number, convert to string, and extend the result list with each digit as an integer.
The order of digits within a number must be preserved.
approach: Iterate through each number in the input array. Convert each number to a string, then iterate through its characters, converting each character back to an integer and appending it to the result list. This preserves the relative order of numbers and digits within each number.
time_complexity_explanation: For n numbers each with at most d digits, we process every digit once, giving O(n*d) total operations.
space_complexity_explanation: The result array holds all digits from all numbers, totaling O(n*d) elements.
key_insight: Converting integers to strings for digit extraction is the most idiomatic and readable approach, avoiding manual modular arithmetic.

title: Corporate Flight Bookings
hints: Use a difference array: for booking [first, last, seats], add seats at index first-1 and subtract at index last.
After processing all bookings, compute the prefix sum of the difference array.
The prefix sum at each index gives the total seats booked for that flight.
approach: Apply the difference array technique. Create an array diff of size n+1. For each booking (first, last, seats), do diff[first-1] += seats and diff[last] -= seats. Then compute the prefix sum of diff, where prefix_sum[i] represents the total seats booked on flight i+1. This achieves O(n+m) time instead of O(n*m) for direct range updates.
time_complexity_explanation: Processing m bookings in the difference array takes O(m), and a single prefix-sum pass over n flights takes O(n).
space_complexity_explanation: The difference array of size n+1 and the result array of size n both use O(n) space.
key_insight: The difference array trick converts O(m) range-update operations from O(n) each to O(1) each, deferring the cost to a single O(n) prefix-sum pass at the end.

title: Convert Object to JSON String
hints: Handle the base cases: null, boolean, number, and string serialize differently from objects and arrays.
For arrays, recursively serialize each element and join with commas inside brackets.
For objects (non-null, non-array), recursively serialize each key-value pair and join inside braces.
approach: Implement a recursive serializer without using JSON.stringify. Check the type of the value: null serializes to 'null'; booleans and numbers serialize to their string form; strings are wrapped in double quotes; arrays serialize each element recursively and join them comma-separated in brackets; plain objects serialize each key as a quoted string followed by a colon and the recursively serialized value, joined comma-separated in braces.
time_complexity_explanation: Each node in the object tree is visited exactly once, so time is proportional to the total number of values n.
space_complexity_explanation: The recursion stack depth is proportional to the maximum nesting depth d of the object structure.
key_insight: The type-checking order matters: check for null before typeof object === 'object', and check Array.isArray before treating a value as a plain object, since arrays are also objects in JavaScript.

title: Check if Array is Good
hints: A good array is a permutation of [1, 2, ..., n-1, n, n] for some n.
The maximum value n must appear exactly twice; all values 1 through n-1 must appear exactly once.
Use a frequency counter and verify the expected counts.
approach: Find the maximum value n in the array. A good array must have length n+1 and be a permutation of [1, 2, ..., n-1, n, n]. Count the frequency of each element: n must appear exactly twice, and every integer from 1 to n-1 must appear exactly once. Any deviation means the array is not good.
time_complexity_explanation: Building the frequency map and verifying each expected value both take O(n) time.
space_complexity_explanation: The frequency map stores at most n distinct values.
key_insight: Knowing that the target is always [1..n-1, n, n] (with n being the array maximum) makes verification straightforward: just check that n appears twice and every smaller positive integer appears once.

title: Minimum Moves to Make Array Complementary
hints: For each pair (nums[i], nums[n-1-i]), figure out the range of target sums T achievable with 0, 1, or 2 moves.
Use a difference array over possible target sums 2..2*limit to record the cost contribution of each pair.
The minimum total cost across all target sums T is the answer.
approach: For each complementary pair (a, b) where a <= b, define: with 0 moves the current sum is a+b; with 1 move the sum can be anything in [1+b, a+limit] (change just a) union [a+1, b+limit] minus the 2-move zone; with 2 moves the sum can be [2, limit+1] or [b+1+a, 2*limit]. Use a difference array of size 2*limit+2 to accumulate the minimum move cost for each possible target sum, then find the minimum prefix sum.
solution_python:
from typing import List

class Solution:
    def minMoves(self, nums: List[int], limit: int) -> int:
        n = len(nums)
        diff = [0] * (2 * limit + 2)
        
        for i in range(n // 2):
            a, b = nums[i], nums[n - 1 - i]
            lo, hi = min(a, b), max(a, b)
            # default: 2 moves for the whole range
            diff[2] += 2
            diff[2 * limit + 1] -= 2
            # 1 move: [lo+1, lo+limit] and [hi+1 ... but simpler: [1+hi, lo+limit]
            # Actually: 1 move suffices in [lo+1, hi+limit]
            diff[lo + 1] -= 1
            diff[hi + limit + 1] += 1
            # 0 moves: exactly a+b
            diff[a + b] -= 1
            diff[a + b + 1] += 1
        
        ans = n
        cur = 0
        for s in range(2, 2 * limit + 1):
            cur += diff[s]
            ans = min(ans, cur)
        return ans
time_complexity_explanation: Processing n/2 pairs into the difference array takes O(n); sweeping the 2*limit range for the minimum takes O(limit).
space_complexity_explanation: The difference array has size 2*limit+2, giving O(limit) space.
key_insight: The difference array over target sums elegantly encodes the piecewise move cost per pair: 2 moves by default across the full range, minus 1 in the achievable-with-one-move range, minus another 1 at the exact current sum requiring 0 moves.

title: Actors and Directors Who Cooperated At Least Three Times
hints: You need to find pairs (actor_id, director_id) that appear together more than twice.
Use GROUP BY on both columns and count the rows in each group.
Filter groups using HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3.
approach: Group the ActorDirector table by (actor_id, director_id) and count the number of times each pair appears. Use HAVING to keep only pairs with a count of at least 3. Select just the actor_id and director_id columns in the result.
solution_python:
SELECT actor_id, director_id
FROM ActorDirector
GROUP BY actor_id, director_id
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3;
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: The database scans all n rows once to build the groups.
space_complexity_explanation: Storage is proportional to k, the number of distinct (actor, director) pairs.
key_insight: A simple GROUP BY with a HAVING filter is all that is needed — no subquery or join required.

title: First Letter to Appear Twice
hints: Iterate through the string character by character.
Keep a set of characters you have already seen.
The first character that is already in the set is your answer.
approach: Iterate through the string one character at a time, maintaining a hash set of characters seen so far. For each character, check if it is already in the set — if so, return it immediately as the answer. Otherwise, add it to the set and continue.
time_complexity_explanation: A single pass through the string of length n is performed.
space_complexity_explanation: The set holds at most 26 lowercase letters, so space is constant.
key_insight: Because the alphabet is small (26 letters), the set can hold at most 26 entries before a duplicate must appear, making this O(1) space.

title: Jewels and Stones
hints: Store all jewel types in a set for O(1) lookups.
Iterate through the stones string and check each character against the set.
Count how many stones are in the jewels set.
approach: Convert the jewels string into a hash set for constant-time lookup. Then iterate over each character in stones and increment a counter whenever the character is found in the jewels set. Return the final count.
time_complexity_explanation: Building the jewel set takes O(j) and scanning all stones takes O(s).
space_complexity_explanation: The hash set stores at most j distinct jewel characters.
key_insight: Converting the jewels string to a set turns each stone lookup from O(j) to O(1), reducing the overall complexity from O(j*s) to O(j+s).

title: Count K-th Roots in a Range
hints: A number x is a perfect k-th power if the k-th root of x is an integer.
Find the smallest and largest integer whose k-th power falls within [lo, hi].
The count is simply the number of integers in that range of roots.
approach: Find the integer k-th root of lo (ceiling) and the integer k-th root of hi (floor). Any integer r in [ceil(lo^(1/k)), floor(hi^(1/k))] satisfies lo <= r^k <= hi. The answer is the count of such integers. Use math.isqrt or careful rounding to avoid floating-point errors for the k-th root.
time_complexity_explanation: Computing the k-th root via binary search or floating-point with adjustment is O(log n) per root computation.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant amount of extra space is used.
key_insight: Rather than enumerating all numbers in [lo, hi], enumerate the possible integer k-th roots — the count of perfect k-th powers in a range equals the count of integers whose k-th power lies in that range.

title: Sum of Subarray Ranges
hints: The range of a subarray is max - min. Sum of ranges = sum of maximums - sum of minimums.
Use a monotonic stack to compute, for each element, how many subarrays it is the maximum of.
Do the same to find how many subarrays each element is the minimum of, then combine.
Contribution of element nums[i] as max: (i - left_bound) * (right_bound - i) * nums[i].
approach: Decompose the problem: sum of ranges = sum of subarray maximums minus sum of subarray minimums. For each element, use a monotonic stack to find the left and right boundaries where it is the maximum (or minimum). Multiply its value by the number of subarrays where it dominates to get its total contribution. Subtract the minimum contribution from the maximum contribution.
time_complexity_explanation: Each element is pushed and popped from the monotonic stack at most once, giving O(n) total work.
space_complexity_explanation: The monotonic stack holds at most n indices at a time.
key_insight: Rewriting sum-of-ranges as (sum of maxes) minus (sum of mins) lets you apply the monotonic stack contribution technique independently to each extreme, keeping both time and code complexity low.

title: Score of a String
hints: Iterate through consecutive pairs of characters.
For each adjacent pair, compute the absolute difference of their ASCII values.
Accumulate the sum of all those differences.
approach: Perform a single linear pass through the string. For each index i from 0 to n-2, compute the absolute difference between the ASCII (ordinal) values of s[i] and s[i+1] and add it to a running total. Return the total after processing all pairs.
time_complexity_explanation: The string is traversed once, visiting each of the n-1 adjacent pairs.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a single accumulator variable is used, requiring O(1) extra space.
key_insight: The score is just the sum of absolute differences of adjacent character codes — a straightforward one-liner that needs no special data structure.

title: Jump Game IV
hints: Model the array as a graph: index i connects to i-1, i+1, and all indices with the same value.
Use BFS from index 0 to find the shortest path to the last index.
Pre-group indices by value to efficiently enumerate same-value jumps, and clear each group once visited to avoid re-processing.
approach: Build a mapping from value to list of indices that share that value. Run BFS from index 0. From each index, enqueue i-1, i+1, and all indices with the same value as arr[i]. To prevent O(n^2) revisiting, remove a value group from the map once it has been processed. The BFS level at which index n-1 is first reached is the answer.
time_complexity_explanation: Each index is enqueued and processed at most once; clearing the value groups ensures same-value edges are visited only once in aggregate.
space_complexity_explanation: The graph mapping and BFS queue each hold O(n) entries in the worst case.
key_insight: Clearing each value's index list from the map after processing it is the critical optimization that prevents the BFS from revisiting the same teleport edges repeatedly, keeping the algorithm O(n) instead of O(n^2).

title: Find the Length of the Longest Common Prefix
hints: For each number in arr1, store all its string prefixes in a set.
For each number in arr2, generate its string prefixes and check if any appear in the set.
Track the maximum matching prefix length found across all pairs.
approach: Convert each number in arr1 to a string and insert all of its prefixes into a hash set. Then, for each number in arr2, generate all of its prefixes and check for membership in the set; track the longest match found. The total work is proportional to the sum of digit lengths across both arrays.
time_complexity_explanation: For each of the m numbers in arr1 with up to d1 digits, and n numbers in arr2 with up to d2 digits, all prefixes are generated — giving O(m*d1 + n*d2) total work.
space_complexity_explanation: The prefix set stores at most m*d1 distinct prefix strings from arr1.
key_insight: Storing all prefixes of arr1 in a hash set transforms each arr2 lookup from a nested O(m) comparison to O(1) per prefix check, enabling an efficient single-pass solution.

title: Find the Prefix Common Array of Two Arrays
hints: At each index i, C[i] is the count of values that have appeared in both A[0..i] and B[0..i].
Maintain two sets (or a frequency array) tracking what each prefix has seen.
When you add A[i] to set A and it is already in set B, increment the common count; same logic for B[i].
approach: Iterate from index 0 to n-1 while maintaining two sets (or a frequency count array). At each step, add A[i] to the first set and B[i] to the second. If A[i] is already in the second set or B[i] is already in the first set, it contributes to the common count. Also handle the case where A[i] == B[i] at the same step to avoid double-counting. Record the running count at each index.
time_complexity_explanation: A single pass through both arrays of length n is performed.
space_complexity_explanation: The frequency array has size n+1, and the output array has size n.
key_insight: Using a single frequency array is cleaner than two sets: a value becomes "common" exactly when its frequency hits 2 (meaning it appeared in both A's prefix and B's prefix), regardless of whether A[i] == B[i].

title: Shortest Path with Alternating Colors
hints: Model the graph with two separate adjacency lists: one for red edges and one for blue edges.
BFS state must track both the current node and the color of the last edge used.
Initialize the queue with node 0 using both possible "last edge" colors to allow the first step to be either color.
approach: Build separate adjacency lists for red and blue edges. Run BFS where each state is (node, last_color_used). Start from node 0 with both color options (meaning the next edge can be either). At each step, if the last edge was red, only traverse blue edges and vice versa. Track visited states as (node, color) pairs to avoid cycles. The BFS distance when a node is first reached (for either color) is its answer.
solution_python:
from typing import List
from collections import defaultdict, deque

class Solution:
    def shortestAlternatingPaths(self, n: int, redEdges: List[List[int]], blueEdges: List[List[int]]) -> List[int]:
        red = defaultdict(list)
        blue = defaultdict(list)
        for u, v in redEdges:
            red[u].append(v)
        for u, v in blueEdges:
            blue[u].append(v)
        
        # state: (node, color) where color 0=red, 1=blue
        dist = [-1] * n
        dist[0] = 0
        visited = set()
        queue = deque()
        queue.append((0, 0, 0))  # (node, steps, last_color)
        queue.append((0, 0, 1))
        visited.add((0, 0))
        visited.add((0, 1))
        
        while queue:
            node, steps, color = queue.popleft()
            neighbors = blue[node] if color == 0 else red[node]
            next_color = 1 - color
            for nb in neighbors:
                if (nb, next_color) not in visited:
                    visited.add((nb, next_color))
                    if dist[nb] == -1:
                        dist[nb] = steps + 1
                    queue.append((nb, steps + 1, next_color))
        
        return dist
time_complexity_explanation: Each (node, color) state is visited at most once; the total states are 2n and total edge traversals are at most 2e where e is the total number of edges.
space_complexity_explanation: The adjacency lists store all e edges and the BFS queue and visited set hold at most O(n + e) entries.
key_insight: The key insight is that BFS state must include the color of the last edge used — without this, you cannot enforce the alternating constraint and may incorrectly merge paths that used different last-edge colors.

title: Find Numbers with Even Number of Digits
hints: Think about how many digits a number has — how can you determine that without converting to a string?
You can use logarithms: the number of digits of n is floor(log10(n)) + 1.
Alternatively, convert each number to a string and check if its length is even — simpler and still O(n).
approach: Iterate through the array and for each number determine the count of its digits. The simplest approach is to convert each number to a string and check if the length is even. Alternatively, use integer division or math.log10 to count digits without string conversion. Count and return how many numbers have an even digit count.
time_complexity_explanation: We iterate through all n numbers once, and converting each number to a string takes O(log n) time, but since numbers are bounded by 10^5 this is effectively O(1) per element.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a counter variable is used; no additional data structures proportional to input size are needed.
key_insight: The simplest and most readable approach is string conversion — checking if len(str(n)) % 2 == 0 is clear and efficient for the given constraints (nums[i] <= 10^5, so at most 6 digits).

title: Make Sum Divisible by P
hints: First compute the total sum modulo p. If it is already 0, the answer is 0 (no removal needed).
You need to remove the smallest subarray whose sum modulo p equals (total_sum % p). Think prefix sums.
Use a hash map to store the most recent index where each prefix sum modulo p was seen, similar to the subarray sum equals k technique.
approach: Compute the total sum mod p; call it target. If target is 0, return 0. Otherwise, find the shortest subarray to remove whose sum mod p equals target. Use prefix sums mod p and a hash map: for each prefix sum cur, check if (cur - target) mod p exists in the map. Track the minimum length of any such subarray found, and return it if it is less than n (we cannot remove the entire array).
time_complexity_explanation: A single pass through the array is made, and each hash map lookup and insertion is O(1) on average.
space_complexity_explanation: The hash map stores at most n distinct prefix sum mod p values, one per index.
key_insight: The problem reduces to finding the shortest subarray with sum congruent to (total % p) mod p — the same prefix-sum-plus-hash-map trick used in "Subarray Sum Equals K" but applied in modular arithmetic.

title: Remove K Digits
hints: Think about which digit to remove first — should you remove from the front or the back of the number?
A monotonic increasing stack helps: whenever a digit is larger than the next one, removing it makes the number smaller.
After processing all digits, if k > 0 still, remove from the end of the stack (the largest trailing digits).
approach: Use a greedy approach with a monotonic increasing stack. Iterate through each digit; while the stack is non-empty, k > 0, and the top of the stack is greater than the current digit, pop the stack and decrement k. Push the current digit. After the loop, if k > 0, remove the last k digits. Strip leading zeros from the result.
time_complexity_explanation: Each digit is pushed and popped from the stack at most once, giving a linear pass over the string.
space_complexity_explanation: The stack stores at most n digits in the worst case.
key_insight: Greedily removing a digit that is larger than the next digit always yields a smaller number — a monotonic increasing stack automates this decision efficiently.

title: Single Number II
hints: Every number except one appears exactly three times — think about what happens to each bit position across all numbers.
If you sum the bits at each position across all numbers, the bits belonging to the tripled numbers contribute a multiple of 3.
Take the bit-sum modulo 3 at each position; the remaining bits reconstruct the single number.
approach: For each of the 32 bit positions, sum the bits of all numbers at that position. Since every number except the answer appears three times, each such bit sum is divisible by 3 if the answer has a 0 there, or leaves remainder 1 if the answer has a 1 there. Taking sum % 3 at each position and reconstructing yields the answer. Alternatively, use two bitmask variables (ones, twos) to track counts mod 3.
time_complexity_explanation: A single pass through the array is made with constant-time bit operations per element.
space_complexity_explanation: Only two integer variables are used regardless of input size.
key_insight: Tracking bit counts modulo 3 with two bitmasks (ones and twos) lets you cancel out all numbers that appear three times, leaving only the unique number.

title: First Bad Version
hints: The versions form a sorted sequence: all good versions come before all bad versions.
Binary search works here — if mid is bad, the first bad version is at mid or earlier; if good, it must be after mid.
Track the leftmost bad version found and narrow the search window accordingly.
approach: Apply binary search over the version range [1, n]. At each midpoint, call isBadVersion(mid). If it returns true, the first bad version is at mid or to the left, so move right to mid. If false, the first bad version is to the right, so move left to mid + 1. Continue until left == right, which is the first bad version.
time_complexity_explanation: Binary search halves the search space at each step, requiring at most log2(n) calls to isBadVersion.
space_complexity_explanation: Only two pointer variables are maintained regardless of n.
key_insight: Because all good versions precede all bad versions, the problem is equivalent to finding the left boundary in a binary search, minimizing the number of expensive API calls.

title: Range Sum Query - Immutable
hints: If sumRange is called many times, recomputing the sum from scratch each time is wasteful.
Precompute a prefix sum array so that sumRange(i, j) can be answered in O(1).
prefix[j+1] - prefix[i] gives the sum of elements from index i to j inclusive.
approach: In the constructor, build a prefix sum array where prefix[i] stores the sum of nums[0..i-1]. Then sumRange(left, right) is simply prefix[right+1] - prefix[left], answering each query in constant time after O(n) preprocessing.
time_complexity_explanation: After O(n) preprocessing, each sumRange query is answered with a single subtraction in O(1).
space_complexity_explanation: The prefix sum array requires O(n) extra space proportional to the input length.
key_insight: Storing prefix sums transforms range sum queries from O(n) repeated work into O(1) lookups by precomputing cumulative totals.

title: Add Digits
hints: Repeatedly summing the digits of any positive integer eventually reaches a single digit — this is the digital root.
There is a direct mathematical formula involving modulo 9 that avoids any looping.
The digital root of n is 0 if n == 0, else 1 + (n - 1) % 9.
approach: Use the digital root formula from number theory. For any positive integer n, the digital root is n % 9 if n % 9 != 0, or 9 if n % 9 == 0 (and n > 0). This reduces the problem to a single arithmetic operation with no loops.
time_complexity_explanation: The digital root formula computes the answer with a fixed number of arithmetic operations regardless of input size.
space_complexity_explanation: No extra data structures are used; only a constant amount of space is needed.
key_insight: The digital root of a number equals its value modulo 9 (with a special case for multiples of 9), because repeatedly summing digits is equivalent to repeatedly subtracting 9.

title: Combinations
hints: Generate all combinations by choosing numbers one at a time from a start index to avoid duplicates.
Use backtracking: add a number to the current combination, recurse, then remove it.
Prune early: if the remaining numbers available are fewer than needed to complete the combination, stop.
approach: Use backtracking to build combinations incrementally. Maintain a current list and a start index. At each step, iterate from start to n, add the candidate to the current list, recurse with start = candidate + 1, then backtrack by removing the candidate. Add the current list to results when its length reaches k.
time_complexity_explanation: There are C(n, k) combinations, and each takes O(k) time to copy into the result.
space_complexity_explanation: The recursion depth is at most k, and the current combination array holds at most k elements.
key_insight: Fixing an ascending order for chosen numbers (always pick from start onward) avoids duplicate combinations without needing a visited set, and pruning stops branches that can no longer reach size k.

title: Substring with Concatenation of All Words
hints: All words have the same length — use that to slice the string into fixed-size word chunks.
For each possible starting offset (0 to word_length - 1), use a sliding window of exactly total_words words.
Maintain a frequency map of the words in the current window; add the new word and remove the oldest word as the window slides.
approach: Since every word has the same length L, iterate over starting offsets 0 through L-1. For each offset, use a sliding window of width total_words * L. Maintain a count map of words in the current window. Expand by adding the next word and shrink from the left when the window has too many of a word or an unknown word appears. Record start indices where the window map matches the target map.
solution_python:
from typing import List
from collections import Counter

class Solution:
    def findSubstring(self, s: str, words: List[str]) -> List[int]:
        if not s or not words:
            return []
        word_len = len(words[0])
        num_words = len(words)
        window_len = word_len * num_words
        word_count = Counter(words)
        result = []

        for offset in range(word_len):
            left = offset
            current = Counter()
            count = 0
            for right in range(offset, len(s) - word_len + 1, word_len):
                word = s[right:right + word_len]
                if word in word_count:
                    current[word] += 1
                    count += 1
                    while current[word] > word_count[word]:
                        left_word = s[left:left + word_len]
                        current[left_word] -= 1
                        count -= 1
                        left += word_len
                    if count == num_words:
                        result.append(left)
                else:
                    current.clear()
                    count = 0
                    left = right + word_len
        return result
time_complexity_explanation: We run L sliding window passes, each scanning O(n/L) words, giving O(n) total work per offset and O(n * L) overall — but effectively O(n) since L is constant.
space_complexity_explanation: The word frequency maps store at most m * L characters, and the result list can hold up to O(n) indices.
key_insight: By iterating over the L possible starting offsets and sliding a word-aligned window, you reduce the problem to a standard sliding window with a frequency map comparison, avoiding O(n * window_size) brute force.

title: Repeated Substring Pattern
hints: If a string s is formed by repeating a substring, then s must be a substring of s + s (excluding the first and last characters).
Alternatively, use the KMP failure function: if the last value of the failure table divides n and n % (n - lps[-1]) == 0, a pattern exists.
The period of the string is n - lps[-1]; if it evenly divides n, the string is a repetition of that period.
approach: Use the doubling trick: concatenate s with itself and check if s appears in the middle of the doubled string (i.e., in (s+s)[1:-1]). This works because any string built from a repeated pattern appears as a rotation within the doubled string. Alternatively, use the KMP failure function to find the minimal period.
time_complexity_explanation: Constructing the doubled string and performing the substring search both take O(n) time with efficient string matching.
space_complexity_explanation: The doubled string requires O(n) extra space.
key_insight: If s is made of k repetitions of a pattern, then s is a rotation of itself; all rotations of s appear as substrings of s+s, so checking membership in (s+s)[1:-1] is a clever O(n) test.

title: Binary Tree Preorder Traversal
hints: Preorder visits the node first, then the left subtree, then the right subtree.
Recursion naturally implements preorder traversal; try implementing it iteratively as a bonus.
For the iterative approach, use a stack — push the right child before the left so the left is processed first.
approach: Traverse the tree in preorder (root, left, right). Recursively: visit the current node, recurse left, recurse right. Iteratively: use an explicit stack; push the root, then repeatedly pop a node, record its value, and push its right then left children (right first so left is processed next).
solution_python:
from typing import List, Optional

# Definition for a binary tree node.
# class TreeNode:
#     def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
#         self.val = val
#         self.left = left
#         self.right = right

class Solution:
    def preorderTraversal(self, root: Optional['TreeNode']) -> List[int]:
        result = []
        stack = [root] if root else []
        while stack:
            node = stack.pop()
            result.append(node.val)
            if node.right:
                stack.append(node.right)
            if node.left:
                stack.append(node.left)
        return result
time_complexity_explanation: Every node is visited exactly once, giving linear time proportional to the number of nodes.
space_complexity_explanation: The stack holds at most O(h) nodes where h is the tree height, which is O(n) in the worst case for a skewed tree.
key_insight: Pushing the right child onto the stack before the left ensures the left subtree is processed first, mirroring recursive preorder without using the call stack.

title: Find Customer Referee
hints: Select customers whose referee_id is not 2 — but be careful about NULL values.
In SQL, NULL compared with any value using = or != returns NULL (not TRUE/FALSE), so NULL != 2 is not TRUE.
Use IS NULL or COALESCE to explicitly handle rows where referee_id is NULL.
approach: Write a SQL SELECT that filters the Customer table for rows where referee_id is not equal to 2. Because referee_id can be NULL and NULL != 2 evaluates to NULL (not TRUE) in SQL, the WHERE clause must also include an IS NULL check using OR to capture customers with no referee.
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: The database scans the Customer table once, which is O(n) in the number of rows.
space_complexity_explanation: The result set stores the names of matching customers, proportional to the number of qualifying rows.
key_insight: NULL values in SQL are not equal to anything, including 2, so a simple != 2 filter silently drops rows where referee_id is NULL — an explicit OR referee_id IS NULL is required.

title: Number of Islands II
hints: Each addLand operation turns a water cell into land — think about how to efficiently merge it with adjacent land cells.
Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union) is ideal: each land cell is a node, and adding land merges it with its neighbors.
When a new cell is added, start with count+1, then for each of the 4 neighbors that is land and in a different component, union them and decrement count.
approach: Use a Union-Find data structure over the m*n grid cells. For each addLand position, mark it as land, increment the island count, then check all 4 neighbors. If a neighbor is already land and belongs to a different component, union the two components and decrement the count. Append the current count after each operation.
time_complexity_explanation: For k operations, each union-find operation is near-constant O(alpha(m*n)) with path compression and union by rank.
space_complexity_explanation: The parent and rank maps store at most m*n cells, one per grid position.
key_insight: Union-Find lets you efficiently track connected components: each addLand potentially merges up to 4 existing islands, and you can update the count in O(alpha) per merge.

title: Count and Say
hints: The sequence is defined recursively: countAndSay(n) is the run-length encoding of countAndSay(n-1).
To generate the next term, scan the current string and count consecutive identical digits.
For each group of consecutive identical digits, append the count followed by the digit to build the next string.
approach: Start with '1' as the base case. For each step up to n, scan the current string using two pointers or a counter to identify runs of identical characters. Build the next string by appending the run length followed by the character for each run. Repeat n-1 times to reach the nth term.
time_complexity_explanation: Each term can be up to twice the length of the previous one, so the nth term has length O(2^n) and we build n-1 terms.
space_complexity_explanation: The longest string stored at any time is the nth term, which can be O(2^n) characters.
key_insight: Each term is a run-length encoding of the previous term; iteratively applying RLE n-1 times starting from '1' produces the answer.

title: Hamming Distance
hints: XOR of two numbers produces a 1-bit wherever the two numbers differ.
After XORing x and y, count the number of 1-bits in the result.
You can count set bits using Python's bin().count('1') or by repeatedly checking and shifting the least significant bit.
approach: XOR x and y to get a number whose set bits correspond to positions where x and y differ. Then count the number of set bits (popcount) in the XOR result. This can be done with built-in bit count functions or by repeatedly extracting the lowest set bit.
time_complexity_explanation: Integers are fixed 32-bit (or bounded) width, so XOR and bit counting run in constant time.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a fixed number of integer variables are used regardless of input size.
key_insight: XOR isolates all differing bits in one operation; counting the 1-bits of the XOR directly gives the Hamming distance.

title: Count Primes
hints: A naive approach checks each number for primality, but that's too slow for large n.
The Sieve of Eratosthenes efficiently marks all composites: start from 2 and mark every multiple.
You only need to sieve up to sqrt(n) since any composite number has a factor no larger than its square root.
approach: Apply the Sieve of Eratosthenes: create a boolean array of size n initialized to True. Starting from 2, for each prime p found, mark all multiples of p (starting from p*p) as composite. Count all indices still marked True at the end (indices 2 through n-1).
time_complexity_explanation: The Sieve of Eratosthenes runs in O(n log log n) time due to the harmonic series of prime reciprocals.
space_complexity_explanation: A boolean array of size n is allocated to track which numbers are prime.
key_insight: Starting the inner loop at p*p (rather than 2*p) is the key optimization: all smaller multiples of p have already been marked by smaller primes.

title: Island Perimeter
hints: Each land cell contributes 4 sides to the perimeter, but shared edges with adjacent land cells reduce the count.
For every pair of horizontally or vertically adjacent land cells, 2 perimeter units are lost (one from each cell).
Count all land cells and all shared edges separately, then compute: perimeter = 4 * land_cells - 2 * shared_edges.
approach: Iterate over every cell in the grid. For each land cell (value 1), add 4 to the perimeter. Then check the cell to the right and the cell below; if either is also land, subtract 2 (one shared edge removes one side from each neighbor). This gives the total perimeter in a single pass.
time_complexity_explanation: Every cell in the m x n grid is visited exactly once.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a single integer accumulator is used; no additional data structures are needed.
key_insight: Rather than tracing boundaries, count each land cell as contributing 4 sides and subtract 2 for every shared edge with a neighbor, reducing the problem to a simple arithmetic scan.

title: Single Number III
hints: XOR all numbers together — the result is xor = a ^ b where a and b are the two unique numbers.
Find any set bit in xor (e.g., the lowest set bit using xor & (-xor)) — this bit differs between a and b.
Use that distinguishing bit to partition all numbers into two groups and XOR each group separately to isolate a and b.
approach: XOR all elements to get a^b (since duplicates cancel). Find a bit that is set in a^b (use lowest set bit: diff & -diff). Partition numbers into two groups based on whether that bit is set. XOR each group separately; duplicates cancel within each group, leaving a in one and b in the other.
time_complexity_explanation: Two linear passes over the array are made; each pass is O(n).
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of integer variables are used regardless of input size.
key_insight: XORing all elements gives a^b; isolating any differing bit lets you split the array into two groups that each XOR to one of the two unique numbers.

title: Convert Sorted List to Binary Search Tree
hints: The middle element of the sorted list should be the root of the BST to keep it height-balanced.
Use the slow/fast pointer technique to find the middle of the linked list in O(n) time.
Recursively build the left subtree from the left half of the list and the right subtree from the right half.
approach: Use the slow/fast pointer technique to find the middle node of the linked list. The middle node becomes the root. Disconnect the left half from the middle, then recursively build the left subtree from the left half and the right subtree from the right half (starting after the middle node). This produces a height-balanced BST.
solution_python:
from typing import Optional

# Definition for singly-linked list.
# class ListNode:
#     def __init__(self, val=0, next=None):
#         self.val = val
#         self.next = next

# Definition for a binary tree node.
# class TreeNode:
#     def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
#         self.val = val
#         self.left = left
#         self.right = right

class Solution:
    def sortedListToBST(self, head: Optional['ListNode']) -> Optional['TreeNode']:
        if not head:
            return None
        if not head.next:
            return TreeNode(head.val)
        prev, slow, fast = None, head, head
        while fast and fast.next:
            prev = slow
            slow = slow.next
            fast = fast.next.next
        prev.next = None  # disconnect left half
        root = TreeNode(slow.val)
        root.left = self.sortedListToBST(head)
        root.right = self.sortedListToBST(slow.next)
        return root
time_complexity_explanation: Finding the middle takes O(n) at each of the O(log n) recursion levels, giving O(n log n) total.
space_complexity_explanation: The recursion stack depth is O(log n) for a balanced BST built from n nodes.
key_insight: Making the middle element the root at each recursive step ensures the BST is height-balanced, since equal-length left and right sublists become equal-depth subtrees.

title: Summary Ranges
hints: Use a pointer to mark the start of each consecutive range, then extend it as long as consecutive elements differ by 1.
When the consecutive sequence breaks (or you reach the end), record the range as either 'a' (single element) or 'a->b' (multi-element).
Move the start pointer to the next element after the break and repeat.
approach: Iterate through the sorted unique array with a start pointer. For each position, advance an end pointer while consecutive elements differ by exactly 1. When the sequence breaks, record either 'start' if start equals end, or 'start->end' otherwise. Move start past the end and continue.
time_complexity_explanation: Each element is visited at most twice (once by the outer loop and once by the inner advance), giving linear time.
space_complexity_explanation: Only pointer variables and the output list are used; the output is not counted as extra space.
key_insight: Tracking the start of each run and extending until a gap is found lets you emit each range in one step without any backtracking.
